proposals.title: A Standardized Benchmark for Self-Supervised Learning in Musculoskeletal MRI: Focus on Patellar Cartilage Segmentation and Denoising
proposals.motivation: Self-supervised learning (SSL) has shown great promise for medical imaging tasks like segmentation and denoising, where labeled data is scarce. However, the field lacks standardized, task-specific benchmarks, leading to unfair comparisons and inflated performance claims that hinder clinical adoption. This problem is acute in musculoskeletal radiology, where methods for structures like the patella vary widely in evaluation protocols.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that establishing a standardized evaluation framework with fixed datasets, pre-processing, and metrics will reveal significant performance differences between leading SSL methods (e.g., SimCLR, BYOL, SwAV) for musculoskeletal MRI tasks, providing clearer guidance for clinical application.
proposals.method: We will curate a multi-institutional knee MRI dataset, focusing on patellar regions from athletes, with expert annotations for cartilage segmentation and paired noisy/clean images for denoising. We will implement a standardized pipeline to train and evaluate multiple SSL pre-training strategies (contrastive, clustering, masked image modeling) followed by supervised fine-tuning for segmentation (U-Net) and a denoising task (using a Noise2Noise-style setup). Performance will be rigorously assessed using fixed metrics (Dice, SSIM, PSNR) and computational efficiency measures on held-out test sets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first publicly available, clinically grounded benchmark for SSL in musculoskeletal MRI, enabling fair comparison and accelerating robust method development. It will deliver concrete recommendations on which SSL pre-training strategies are most effective for segmentation and denoising of small, complex structures like patellar cartilage.
proposals.novelty: While general SSL benchmarks exist, none are tailored to the specific challenges of musculoskeletal imaging and patellar analysis. This proposal uniquely integrates the researcher's expertise in patellar imaging, segmentation, and denoising to create a domain-specific evaluation standard, moving beyond generic computer vision comparisons.

proposals.title: Patella-Focused Foundation Models for Musculoskeletal MRI: Self-Supervised Pre-training on Multi-Institutional Datasets for Enhanced Segmentation and Injury Detection
proposals.motivation: Current deep learning models for musculoskeletal (MSK) imaging, particularly for patellar and knee joint analysis, are typically narrow, task-specific, and require extensive labeled data, limiting their clinical utility and generalizability. The success of foundation models in natural language processing and computer vision suggests a transformative opportunity for medical imaging, where a single, large-scale pre-trained model could serve as a versatile foundation for diverse downstream tasks like segmentation, denoising, and pathology detection with minimal fine-tuning.
proposals.hypothesis: A vision transformer (ViT) or masked autoencoder (MAE) pre-trained via self-supervised learning on a massive, diverse corpus of unlabeled knee MRI scans will learn robust, transferable representations of MSK anatomy, leading to superior performance and data efficiency on supervised downstream tasks like patellar cartilage segmentation and injury pattern classification compared to models trained from scratch.
proposals.method: We will curate a large-scale, multi-institutional dataset of knee MRI sequences (e.g., PD, T1, T2). A Vision Transformer (ViT-Large) or a Masked Autoencoder (MAE) architecture will be pre-trained using a self-supervised objective, such as contrastive learning (SimCLR, MoCo v3) or masked image modeling, on this unlabeled data. This foundation model will then be fine-tuned on smaller, labeled datasets for specific supervised tasks central to Dr. Chaudhari's work: automatic cartilage quantification (segmentation) and detection of valgus extension overload-related patellar adaptations (classification), leveraging recent advancements in transfer learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver one of the first large-scale, publicly available foundation models specifically for MSK MRI, providing a powerful pre-trained backbone for the research community. It will demonstrate a significant reduction in labeled data requirements and performance gains for critical tasks in sports medicine radiology, directly advancing automated injury analysis and cartilage monitoring for athletes.
proposals.novelty: While foundation models are emerging in general medical imaging (e.g., chest X-ray), a dedicated model for the nuanced domain of musculoskeletal MRI, particularly one focused on the patellofemoral joint and pre-trained with modern SSL techniques on a scale previously unseen in this subfield, represents a novel and targeted advancement beyond generic models or small-scale task-specific networks.

proposals.title: Active Learning for Joint Noise-Agnostic Restoration of Knee MRI: A Framework for Efficient, Patient-Adaptive Denoising in Sports Medicine
proposals.motivation: In sports medicine radiology, particularly for knee imaging in athletes, image quality is degraded by heterogeneous noise from fast acquisition protocols and patient-specific factors, complicating diagnosis of subtle patellar and cartilage injuries. Current supervised deep learning denoisers require extensive, pixel-level clean labels and assume a known, uniform noise model, which is impractical and inaccurate for real-world clinical data. An efficient method to learn from limited expert feedback and adapt to variable noise is needed to improve quantitative cartilage segmentation and injury pattern analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an active learning framework which jointly estimates a clean image and a latent noise representation, and strategically queries experts for low-cost feedback on high-uncertainty cases, can efficiently bootstrap a high-performance, noise-agnostic denoiser tailored to the heterogeneous imaging cohort in sports medicine.
proposals.method: We will develop a joint restoration model with a U-Net-based architecture featuring a dual-branch decoder: one branch outputs the clean image estimate, while the other estimates a latent noise map. Model uncertainty will be quantified using Monte Carlo dropout. An active learning loop, built on a pre-2026 query strategy like Bayesian Active Learning by Disagreement (BALD), will select scans where the clean and noise estimates are most uncertain. For these scans, expert radiologists will provide targeted feedback, such as a quality score for a specific region-of-interest (e.g., the patellofemoral joint) or a small, clean patch annotation, rather than a full clean image.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel active learning protocol for medical image restoration that drastically reduces expert annotation burden by leveraging sparse, targeted feedback, and 2) a joint estimation model that provides patient-adaptive denoising and a data-driven characterization of the clinical noise landscape, directly benefiting downstream tasks like cartilage quantification and injury assessment in athletes.
proposals.novelty: The approach inverts the standard paradigm by using active learning to refine a joint image-and-noise model, rather than to collect full clean labels for a fixed-noise denoiser. The query for sparse, clinically expedient feedback on uncertainty, instead of pixel-perfect labels, is a novel strategy for bootstrapping restoration models in resource-constrained medical domains.

proposals.title: Certifiably Safe Acceleration of Latent Diffusion Models for Musculoskeletal MRI via Constrained Adversarial Distillation
proposals.motivation: Diffusion models show great promise for medical image reconstruction and synthesis, but their slow inference is a major barrier to clinical adoption. Standard acceleration techniques, such as knowledge distillation or reduced sampling steps, risk degrading diagnostic fidelity, especially on subtle pathologies like cartilage lesions or patellar tendon injuries common in athletes. There is a critical need for acceleration methods that provide formal, validated guarantees of performance preservation for specific clinical tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formulating diffusion model acceleration as a constrained optimization problem—minimizing inference time subject to a hard diagnostic fidelity constraint—we can produce clinically deployable models with certified performance bounds, without sacrificing robustness on edge-case medical images.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework combining adaptive knowledge distillation from a pre-trained latent diffusion model (e.g., Stable Diffusion architecture) into a more efficient student network. The core innovation is a constrained optimization loop using a Lagrange multiplier, where the loss function penalizes deviation from a pre-defined minimum performance threshold on a curated adversarial validation set. This set will include challenging cases from knee MRI (e.g., from the IWOAI challenge) featuring early osteoarthritis, complex meniscal tears, and patellar maltracking. We will integrate efficient samplers (e.g., DDIM, DPM-Solver) and continuously validate against fidelity metrics like lesion detection sensitivity and segmentation Dice score.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel constrained optimization framework for clinically safe model acceleration, 2) accelerated diffusion models for musculoskeletal MRI with attached performance certificates, and 3) a publicly released adversarial dataset of knee imaging edge cases to benchmark robustness. This moves beyond reporting average performance to providing actionable safety assurances for deployment.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that trades off speed and quality post-hoc, our method enforces diagnostic fidelity as a hard constraint during the acceleration process itself, and certifies robustness via continuous validation against a targeted adversarial dataset of clinical edge cases.

proposals.title: A Multi-Pathology Benchmark for Clinical Robustness: Validating Supervised Denoising Networks on a Standardized Musculoskeletal MRI Test Suite
proposals.motivation: Current deep learning-based medical image denoising methods are often validated on limited, pathology-specific datasets or generic image quality metrics. This fails to prove their robustness across the diverse pathologies and scanner variations encountered in real-world radiology, particularly in complex musculoskeletal (MSK) imaging where subtle features are critical for diagnosis. There is a pressing need, analogous to the ImageNet benchmark in computer vision, for a standardized, multi-pathology test suite to rigorously evaluate clinical utility and prevent overfitting to narrow tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a supervised denoising method, systematically evaluated on a comprehensive, multi-pathology benchmark, will demonstrate variable performance across different clinical conditions, and that its robustness can be quantified by pathology-specific evaluation metrics that correlate with diagnostic interpretability.
proposals.method: We will curate a benchmark dataset from public and institutional MSK MRI repositories, encompassing pathologies relevant to Dr. Chaudhari's work (e.g., patellar tendinopathy, cartilage lesions, ACL tears) and artifacts from various scanner models. A state-of-the-art supervised denoising network, such as a Noise2Noise or RED-CNN variant, will be trained on a separate, diverse dataset. Its performance will be rigorously tested on the benchmark suite using metrics beyond PSNR/SSIM, including task-specific measures like cartilage segmentation accuracy (DICE) on post-denoised images and the preservation of texture features known to be diagnostically relevant for each pathology.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a publicly available, standardized test suite for clinical denoising validation, moving beyond single-disease evaluations. It will establish a framework for quantifying the clinical robustness of denoising algorithms and produce specific evidence on which pathologies and imaging conditions are most challenging for current supervised methods.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting the computer vision benchmarking paradigm to create a pathology-aware evaluation framework for medical denoising, shifting the focus from general image quality to clinically grounded, diagnostic-task preservation across a broad disease spectrum.

proposals.title: Clinically-Optimized Score-Based Diffusion for Knee MRI Reconstruction: Enhancing Patellofemoral Pathology Detection
proposals.motivation: Current deep learning methods for MRI reconstruction, including supervised and diffusion models, are optimized for pixel-level metrics like PSNR or SSIM. However, these metrics do not guarantee optimal performance for specific clinical tasks, such as detecting subtle cartilage lesions or patellar maltracking in athletes. A reconstruction method that directly optimizes for diagnostic accuracy could improve clinical confidence and outcomes, particularly in sports medicine where imaging quality is critical for injury assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: A score-based stochastic differential equation (SDE) framework for MRI reconstruction, where the reverse diffusion process is trained end-to-end to maximize the accuracy of a downstream clinical task network (e.g., cartilage defect segmentation or patellar instability classification), will produce reconstructed images with superior diagnostic utility compared to reconstructions optimized for traditional fidelity metrics.
proposals.method: We will adapt the continuous-time score-based SDE framework (Song et al., 2021) for accelerated knee MRI reconstruction. The forward SDE will define a controlled corruption of fully-sampled k-space data. The reverse SDE, parameterized by a time-conditional U-Net, will be trained using a combined loss: the standard score-matching loss and a task-specific loss from a pre-trained, frozen clinical predictor network (e.g., a segmentation model for femoral trochlear cartilage). This predictor will provide gradients to steer the reconstruction to preserve or enhance features critical for diagnosis, using backpropagation through the differentiable sampling process.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, clinically-driven image reconstruction paradigm that explicitly bridges the gap between image quality metrics and diagnostic performance. It will provide a new validation framework for reconstruction algorithms in medical imaging. The resulting method is expected to demonstrate tangible improvements in detecting patellofemoral pathologies in datasets of overhead athletes, directly supporting Dr. Chaudhari's research themes.
proposals.novelty: While diffusion models have been explored for medical image reconstruction, inverting the validation paradigm to train the generative model directly for downstream clinical task performance—rather than image fidelity—is a novel and non-obvious application. This moves beyond using a clinical task merely as an external evaluation metric to making it the core optimization objective of the reconstruction process itself.

proposals.title: Robust Single-Scan Denoising for Knee MRI via Test-Time Adaptation with a Generative Anatomical Prior
proposals.motivation: Supervised deep learning models for medical image denoising, such as those applied to knee MRI for cartilage quantification or patellar injury assessment, degrade when test data deviates from the training distribution due to scanner-specific noise or artifacts. This is a critical barrier for deploying robust models in clinical and sports medicine settings, where scan protocols vary. Current test-time adaptation (TTA) methods risk catastrophic overfitting to a single noisy input, corrupting anatomical structures essential for diagnosis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that constraining TTA updates via a frozen generative model, which encodes a prior distribution of clean knee anatomy, will enable effective scan-specific denoising while preventing anatomically implausible outputs, thereby improving robustness across diverse clinical and portable imaging setups.
proposals.method: We will develop a TTA framework where a pre-trained denoising network (e.g., a U-Net) is adapted on a single noisy knee MRI scan. The adaptation loss will combine a standard reconstruction term (e.g., mean squared error) with a regularization term derived from a pre-trained generative adversarial network (GAN) or diffusion model, such as StyleGAN2-ADA, trained on clean knee MRI patches. This regularization, implemented via a latent space projection or feature matching loss, will penalize outputs that deviate from the clean anatomical manifold. The adaptation will use gradient-based updates, with the generative model kept frozen to maintain a stable prior.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, robust denoising method that safely adapts to individual scans without requiring re-training or large test datasets. It will provide a principled framework for integrating anatomical priors into TTA, directly benefiting downstream tasks like cartilage segmentation and injury pattern analysis in athletes. The method will be validated on knee MRI datasets with simulated and real-world noise.
proposals.novelty: While TTA and generative priors exist separately, their integration to explicitly bound anatomical plausibility during single-scan adaptation is novel. This approach moves beyond simple consistency or entropy minimization in TTA by leveraging a rich, data-driven prior of healthy and pathological knee anatomy to prevent catastrophic failure.

proposals.title: A Pathology-Conditioned Flow Matching Model for Synthesizing Knee MRI Across Injury Severity Spectra
proposals.motivation: Supervised deep learning for musculoskeletal imaging, such as patellar injury assessment or cartilage segmentation, is limited by scarce, imbalanced data for rare pathologies and atypical disease progressions. Existing generative models like GANs or diffusion models often lack precise, invertible control over pathology severity, hindering their use for robust data augmentation and interpretable progression visualization in clinical validation studies.
proposals.hypothesis: A continuous normalizing flow (CNF) model, conditioned on anatomical context and a continuous pathology severity descriptor, can learn smooth, semantically meaningful trajectories in image space to accurately synthesize medical images across a spectrum from normal to diseased states for multiple knee conditions.
proposals.method: We will develop a flow matching framework (Lipman et al., 2022) where a neural ODE defines a CNF. The vector field will be parameterized by a U-Net conditioned via adaptive group normalization on: 1) an anatomical segmentation mask (e.g., patella, cartilage from prior work), and 2) a continuous, multi-dimensional pathology descriptor encoding condition type (e.g., valgus overload, ACL tear) and severity. Training will use paired image-pathology datasets (e.g., MRIs with radiologist severity scores) to regress the conditional vector field, enabling deterministic sampling and inversion.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a fast, high-fidelity synthesis tool for augmenting rare injury datasets in sports medicine radiology. The model's deterministic invertibility offers a novel platform for visualizing quantified disease progression pathways and conducting controlled simulation studies for injury prevention and rehabilitation method validation.
proposals.novelty: This integrates the precise control and invertibility of flow matching with multi-pathology severity conditioning, moving beyond single-disease or discrete-label synthesis to create a unified, interpretable model for generating the continuous pathological spectrum observed in clinical practice, unlike prevailing diffusion or GAN approaches.

proposals.title: A Task-Specific, Supervised Learning Framework for Selective Knee MRI Segmentation and Pathology Detection in Overhead Athletes
proposals.motivation: Standard knee MRI protocols generate comprehensive 3D volumes, but radiologists often focus on specific structures (e.g., patellar cartilage, ACL) based on the clinical scenario, such as valgus extension overload in overhead athletes. This comprehensive evaluation is time-consuming and computationally expensive for automated analysis. A paradigm shift towards task-specific, streamlined analysis could improve efficiency and clinical relevance.
proposals.hypothesis: A deep learning model trained to selectively segment and evaluate only the knee structures most relevant to a specific clinical question (e.g., patellofemoral pain in throwers) will achieve diagnostic accuracy comparable to a full-model evaluation while significantly reducing computational cost and analysis time.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-task, supervised learning framework using a shared encoder with task-specific decoder branches. Based on pre-2026 architectures like nnU-Net, the model will be trained on a curated dataset of knee MRIs from overhead athletes, with annotations for all major structures. A clinical question classifier (e.g., 'pain location: anterior') will act as a gate, activating only the relevant decoder (e.g., for patella, trochlea, fat pad). We will incorporate advanced denoising techniques (e.g., based on Noise2Void or supervised denoising CNNs) as a pre-processing step to improve segmentation accuracy on noisy clinical scans.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a proof-of-concept for 'clinical question-driven' AI analysis in musculoskeletal radiology, moving beyond generic full-segmentation models. It will deliver a more efficient tool for targeted diagnosis in sports medicine and validate the principle of selective evaluation, potentially reducing radiologist workload and AI compute requirements.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., the IWOAI challenge) focuses on comprehensive segmentation, this proposal innovates by dynamically restricting the anatomical scope of analysis based on a simulated clinical input, integrating diagnostic gating directly into the segmentation pipeline for knee MRI.

proposals.title: Longitudinal, Supervised Deep Learning for qDESS-Based Patellar Cartilage T2 Mapping in Overhead Athletes: A Method for Denoising and Quantifying Degeneration
proposals.motivation: Longitudinal monitoring of patellar cartilage in overhead athletes is critical for understanding early degeneration linked to valgus extension overload, but conventional T2 mapping sequences are time-prohibitive for clinical use. The qDESS (quantitative Double-Echo Steady-State) sequence offers a rapid, high-resolution alternative for T2 quantification, yet its derived maps can be noisy, complicating precise tracking of subtle changes over time. Robust, automated methods are needed to enable reliable, out-of-lab assessment of cartilage health in athletic populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a supervised deep learning model, trained on paired noisy qDESS-derived T2 maps and high-quality reference T2 maps (e.g., from multi-echo spin echo), can effectively denoise and segment patellar cartilage, producing precise longitudinal T2 metrics that correlate with clinical outcomes and injury risk in overhead throwing athletes.
proposals.method: We will employ a supervised learning framework using a U-Net architecture, a proven method for medical image segmentation and enhancement. The model will be trained on a dataset of knee MRI scans from athletes, with inputs being qDESS-derived T2 maps and outputs being both denoised T2 maps and corresponding cartilage segmentations, using expert-annotated multi-echo spin echo T2 maps as ground truth. The trained pipeline will be applied to longitudinal scans from a cohort of overhead athletes to automatically extract mean T2 values and texture features from the patellar cartilage over time, assessing rates of change.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a fully automated, denoising-integrated pipeline for longitudinal qDESS cartilage analysis, specifically tailored to the patellofemoral joint in athletes. It will provide a validated, efficient tool for clinical trials and injury prevention studies, moving beyond lab-based segmentation challenges to practical, noisy, longitudinal data. The resulting longitudinal T2 trajectories will offer new biomarkers for early cartilage degeneration related to specific throwing mechanics.
proposals.novelty: While qDESS for cartilage is known, the integration of a supervised denoising-segmentation network specifically for noisy, longitudinal qDESS patellar data in a defined athletic population with unique injury patterns (valgus overload) is novel. This method directly addresses the gap between portable sensing for prevention and the need for robust, quantitative imaging biomarkers in out-of-lab monitoring.

proposals.title: A Multi-Task Deep Learning Framework for Automated Detection and Grading of Cartilage Pathology from Quantitative Double-Echo Steady-State (qDESS) MRI
proposals.motivation: Quantitative MRI sequences like qDESS, which simultaneously provides morphologic and T2 relaxation time data, are promising for osteoarthritis (OA) assessment but require expert, time-consuming analysis. Automated tools for pathology detection are needed to translate these advanced sequences into efficient clinical workflows, particularly for longitudinal monitoring in high-risk populations like overhead throwing athletes, where subtle early cartilage changes are common.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-task convolutional neural network (CNN), trained on paired qDESS images and derived T2 maps, can accurately and simultaneously localize, segment, and grade focal cartilage lesions, outperforming models trained on single-contrast data.
proposals.method: We will develop a supervised deep learning model based on a 3D U-Net architecture with multi-task output heads. The network will be trained on a dataset of knee MRI scans (qDESS and T2 maps) from athletes, with expert annotations for cartilage segmentation (from prior work) and pixel-wise pathology labels (e.g., ICRS grades). We will integrate a dedicated denoising module, inspired by Noise2Noise or a U-Net-based autoencoder, to pre-process the qDESS images, improving model robustness. Performance will be validated against expert radiologist readings for detection and grading accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a fully automated pipeline for quantitative cartilage pathology assessment from a single, efficient MRI acquisition (qDESS). It will provide a validated tool for objective, rapid evaluation of early degenerative changes, directly supporting clinical trials and personalized rehabilitation strategies in sports medicine.
proposals.novelty: While segmentation from qDESS has been explored, the integrated, multi-task detection and grading of pathology directly from the combined qDESS/T2 data stream, enhanced by a tailored denoising approach, is a novel application. This moves beyond simple cartilage quantification to direct diagnostic aid.

proposals.title: Deep Learning for Patellar Tendinopathy Risk Stratification in Athletes Using MRI Alone: A Denoising and Segmentation Pipeline
proposals.motivation: Clinical risk factors like training load or prior injury history are often inconsistently recorded in athlete health records, limiting the deployment of predictive models in real-world sports medicine. Relying solely on imaging data, which is routinely acquired for diagnosis, could enable more scalable and objective risk assessment. This is particularly relevant for patellar tendinopathy, a common and debilitating condition in overhead and jumping athletes where early, image-based detection could guide prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a supervised deep learning model, trained exclusively on quantitative features extracted from knee MRI sequences, can achieve performance comparable to models that integrate clinical variables for predicting patellar tendinopathy risk.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage pipeline. First, a U-Net variant will be trained for robust, automatic segmentation of the patellar tendon and surrounding structures from T1/T2-weighted and PD MRI, building on prior segmentation challenge methodologies. Second, a 3D convolutional neural network (CNN) or Vision Transformer will be trained for classification, using the segmented tendon volumes as primary input. To address image quality variability common in clinical data, we will integrate a self-supervised denoising step (e.g., using Noise2Void or a denoising autoencoder) as a pre-processing module within the training pipeline.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a fully automated, imaging-only tool for patellar tendon risk assessment, directly applicable in radiology workflows. It will provide evidence for the sufficiency of imaging biomarkers when clinical data is missing, advancing the field towards more robust and deployable AI in sports medicine. The integrated denoising and segmentation approach aims to improve model generalizability across different scanner protocols.
proposals.novelty: While prior work segments cartilage for OA, applying a similar integrated pipeline specifically for the patellar tendon and for injury prediction (rather than quantification) is novel. The explicit focus on eliminating dependency on clinical covariates by intensively mining imaging data distinguishes this from most existing sports injury prediction models that are multi-modal.

proposals.title: Multi-Modal Feature Fusion for Enhanced Patellofemoral Joint Assessment: A Deep Learning Framework Integrating MRI and X-ray Data
proposals.motivation: Accurate assessment of patellofemoral joint health, particularly in athletes prone to valgus extension overload, requires comprehensive evaluation of both bone alignment and soft-tissue integrity. Current clinical practice relies on separate analysis of X-ray (for bony anatomy) and MRI (for cartilage and soft tissues), which can lead to fragmented diagnostics. A unified, data-driven model that fuses these complementary modalities could provide a more holistic view of injury risk and progression, directly addressing challenges in athlete monitoring and cartilage quantification highlighted in recent reviews.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a deep learning model performing early, feature-level fusion of X-ray and MRI data will outperform single-modality and late-fusion models in tasks critical to patellofemoral assessment, specifically in the simultaneous segmentation of patellar cartilage and quantification of alignment metrics related to injury risk.
proposals.method: We will develop a supervised, multi-stream convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture. The model will feature two encoder branches: one for high-resolution knee X-rays (emphasizing bony edges via a U-Net with residual blocks) and one for MRI sequences (e.g., fat-saturated PD-weighted, using a similar encoder for cartilage texture). Feature maps from intermediate layers of both encoders will be fused via concatenation and processed through cross-modal attention gates before a shared decoder performs joint segmentation of patellar cartilage and key bony landmarks. Training will leverage existing segmented datasets from challenges like IWOAI, with data augmentation and denoising techniques applied to simulate clinical variability.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, interpretable multi-modal fusion framework specifically tailored for musculoskeletal imaging of the knee. It will provide a validated tool for automated, integrated analysis of bone alignment and cartilage health, advancing towards portable injury prevention systems. The resulting model and fusion strategy will be a methodological contribution applicable to other orthopedic multi-modal diagnostic problems.
proposals.novelty: While multi-modal learning exists, an early feature-level fusion strategy specifically designed for the structurally complementary but fundamentally different data of X-ray (2D projection) and MRI (3D volume) for patellofemoral analysis is novel. The integration of attention mechanisms to weight cross-modal features based on spatial context for this clinical problem moves beyond simple concatenation or late fusion common in pre-2026 work.

proposals.title: Attention-Guided Knee Joint Analysis: A Vision Transformer for Multi-Structure Segmentation and Injury Pattern Detection in Athlete MRI
proposals.motivation: Accurate segmentation of knee structures (patellar cartilage, ligaments, menisci) from MRI is critical for quantifying injury patterns in athletes, as seen in Dr. Chaudhari's work on valgus extension overload. However, existing convolutional neural networks (CNNs) struggle to model the complex, long-range anatomical dependencies between these structures, limiting the precision of automated quantification for clinical trials and injury prevention studies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture, leveraging self-attention mechanisms, will outperform state-of-the-art CNNs in multi-structure knee MRI segmentation by effectively capturing global contextual relationships, leading to more accurate detection of subtle injury-related morphological changes.
proposals.method: We will adapt a standard Vision Transformer (ViT-B/16) pre-trained on ImageNet for 2D knee MRI slices. The model will be fine-tuned on a curated dataset of athlete knee MRIs with manual annotations for patellar cartilage, ACL, menisci, and bone. We will implement a hybrid CNN-ViT encoder to extract local features before the transformer blocks. Training will use a supervised learning framework with a combined Dice and cross-entropy loss, and performance will be benchmarked against a U-Net baseline on metrics of segmentation accuracy (Dice Score) and clinical relevance in identifying injury patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, attention-based tool for precise, automated knee joint quantification, directly supporting clinical trials and biomechanical studies of athlete injury. It will establish a benchmark for transformer-based models in musculoskeletal radiology and release a publicly available implementation to advance the field.
proposals.novelty: While transformers dominate natural language processing, their application to knee MRI segmentation for sports injury analysis is nascent. This proposal uniquely integrates ViTs with Dr. Chaudhari's expertise in athlete-specific knee imaging and denoising to model global anatomical context, moving beyond the local receptive fields of CNNs commonly used in prior segmentation challenges.

proposals.title: A Supervised Deep Learning Model for Predicting Patellofemoral Cartilage Health from Low-Cost, Single-View Knee Radiographs
proposals.motivation: MRI is the gold standard for assessing patellofemoral cartilage health and injury risk in athletes, but it is costly and inaccessible in many settings. Plain radiographs are far more ubiquitous and affordable, yet they are considered insufficient for soft-tissue evaluation. Developing a method to extract predictive biomarkers of cartilage status from X-rays would democratize advanced musculoskeletal screening, particularly for overhead athletes and ACL rehabilitation cohorts where patellar tracking is critical.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a supervised deep learning model can learn latent representations in standard knee radiographs that are predictive of patellofemoral cartilage quality and injury patterns, as defined by paired MRI ground truth, enabling a low-cost screening tool.
proposals.method: We will curate a dataset of paired AP/lateral knee radiographs and corresponding MRI scans from athletes, leveraging existing segmentation challenge data for cartilage masks. A dual-stream convolutional neural network (CNN), based on a pre-trained ResNet-50 architecture, will process the X-ray views. The model will be trained in a supervised manner using the MRI-derived cartilage thickness maps and clinical injury labels as targets. Advanced data augmentation and noise injection techniques, informed by Dr. Chaudhari's denoising expertise, will be used to improve robustness and generalizability from limited paired data.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first validated model for predicting patellofemoral cartilage metrics from radiographs alone, creating a practical tool for large-scale athlete screening and longitudinal monitoring in resource-constrained environments. It will also contribute a novel method for cross-modality representation learning, translating expensive 3D biomarkers to 2D imaging.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on segmenting bone in X-rays or cartilage in MRI, this directly bridges the modalities by predicting detailed cartilage health from a low-cost scan, a task previously deemed infeasible. The integration of denoising methods to handle radiographic variability and the focus on the patellofemoral joint in athletes address a specific clinical gap.

proposals.title: Self-Supervised Denoising of Low-Count Knee MRI via Noise2Noise and Anatomical Consistency for Patellar Imaging in Athletes
proposals.motivation: In musculoskeletal MRI for athletes, particularly for patellar tracking and cartilage assessment, reducing scan time and radiation dose (in CT) is crucial for patient comfort and feasibility in longitudinal studies. Current deep learning denoising methods predominantly require paired low- and high-quality scans for supervised training, which are difficult and expensive to acquire in clinical practice, creating a bottleneck for method deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: A self-supervised learning framework, leveraging the Noise2Noise principle and domain-specific anatomical constraints, can learn an effective denoising function from low-count MRI data alone, performing comparably to models trained with paired standard-dose references.
proposals.method: We will develop a method based on the Noise2Noise framework, where the network learns to map from one noisy instance of a low-count scan to another. For 3D knee MRI (e.g., from the IWOAI challenge dataset), we will use a 3D U-Net architecture. To incorporate anatomical prior knowledge and improve stability for very low-count data, we will integrate a consistency loss that penalizes deviations from plausible cartilage and patellar bone shapes, using a pre-trained, frozen segmentation network to guide feature-space alignment without needing clean labels.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical denoising pipeline that eliminates the need for curated paired datasets, accelerating the application of low-dose imaging in sports medicine. It will deliver a validated model for enhancing patellar and cartilage image quality from rapid scans, directly supporting injury prevention and rehabilitation research as outlined in the researcher's prior scoping review.
proposals.novelty: While Noise2Noise has been explored in general imaging, its application to low-count musculoskeletal MRI, specifically augmented with task-specific anatomical consistency losses for cartilage and patellar structures without any clean data, is novel and addresses a key logistical hurdle in clinical research.

proposals.title: DeepVideoBio: A Supervised Learning Framework for Direct Patellofemoral Kinematic and Stress Prediction from Portable Video in Athletic Populations
proposals.motivation: Current biomechanical analysis for patellofemoral pain and injury risk in athletes relies on complex, lab-based motion capture and computationally expensive musculoskeletal (MSK) modeling, limiting accessibility for widespread screening. Portable video offers a scalable alternative, but existing methods require explicit 3D reconstruction and physics simulation, introducing error propagation and latency. A direct mapping from video to dynamics would enable real-time, field-based assessment of patellar tracking and joint loading.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a deep neural network can directly and accurately infer key patellofemoral kinematics and relative tissue stress metrics from monocular video sequences of athletic movements, bypassing explicit MSK modeling without significant loss of predictive validity compared to gold-standard lab measurements.
proposals.method: We will develop a supervised spatiotemporal convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture, likely a 3D CNN or a CNN-LSTM hybrid, trained on a paired dataset of synchronized 2D video and 3D kinematics/kinetics from lab-based motion capture and MRI-derived subject-specific MSK models (as used in prior patellar studies). The model will be trained to regress to target variables including patellar tilt, shift, and estimated patellofemoral contact force. We will employ advanced denoising and segmentation techniques from Chaudhari's prior work to pre-process video and isolate the knee region of interest, improving model focus and robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel end-to-end deep learning pipeline for video-based biomechanical analysis, 2) validation of a practical, low-cost tool for assessing patellofemoral dynamics relevant to valgus overload and injury in overhead athletes, and 3) a foundational framework that extends portable sensing from injury prevention scouting towards quantitative, dynamic joint-level assessment.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in completely bypassing the intermediate physics-based simulation pipeline (inverse kinematics, inverse dynamics, MSK model solving) to learn a direct video-to-dynamics mapping for a specific, clinically complex joint, leveraging supervised learning on a fused imaging-biomechanics dataset rather than relying on generative or unsupervised pose estimation methods.

proposals.title: Bayesian Deep Learning for Uncertainty-Aware Quantification of Patellar Cartilage Thickness in Overhead Athletes
proposals.motivation: Quantitative MRI-based cartilage thickness is a critical biomechanical measure for assessing patellar health and injury risk in overhead athletes, a population studied by Chaudhari et al. (pre-2026). Current supervised deep learning segmentation methods for cartilage quantification provide point estimates but lack confidence intervals, limiting clinical interpretability. This is problematic for longitudinal monitoring and clinical trials where understanding measurement reliability is essential for decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Bayesian deep learning framework, by modeling epistemic (model) and aleatoric (data) uncertainty, can produce accurate, voxel-wise confidence intervals for predicted patellar cartilage thickness maps from MRI, and that the magnitude of this uncertainty will correlate with regions of clinical concern, such as areas of early degeneration.
proposals.method: We will extend Chaudhari's prior segmentation work by implementing a probabilistic U-Net architecture with Monte Carlo dropout at test time to estimate epistemic uncertainty. We will model aleatoric uncertainty using a heteroscedastic noise model within the loss function. The model will be trained on a curated dataset of knee MRI scans from overhead athletes, with ground truth segmentations from the IWOAI challenge lineage. Predictions will yield per-voxel thickness maps with associated 95% credible intervals.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first uncertainty-aware pipeline for patellar cartilage quantification tailored to athletic populations. It will deliver a clinically interpretable tool that highlights regions of low confidence, potentially flagging early pathological changes or image artifacts. The method will advance the field from deterministic measurements towards reliable, probabilistic biomarkers for injury prevention and rehabilitation studies.
proposals.novelty: While Bayesian deep learning has been explored in general medical imaging, its application to provide spatially-resolved confidence intervals for biomechanical measures derived from segmentation (like cartilage thickness) in a specific, high-injury-risk athletic cohort is novel. This integrates probabilistic ML directly into the clinical biomechanics workflow for patellar analysis.

proposals.title: Subject-Specific Patellofemoral Parameter Estimation via Deep Learning-Based Inverse Dynamics from Wearable Sensor Data
proposals.motivation: Personalized musculoskeletal models are critical for understanding injury mechanisms, such as patellofemoral pain in overhead athletes, but obtaining subject-specific anatomical parameters (e.g., patellar tendon moment arm, quadriceps line of action) typically requires costly medical imaging. This limits widespread application in sports science and rehabilitation. A method to infer these parameters from readily captured movement data would enable personalized biomechanical analysis outside the lab, directly addressing needs identified in portable sensing reviews for ACL and other knee injury prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a supervised deep learning model, trained on paired biomechanical simulation data, can accurately infer key subject-specific patellofemoral parameters from time-series kinematic and estimated load data captured via wearable sensors.
proposals.method: We will generate a large synthetic dataset using OpenSim to simulate movements (e.g., squats, jumps) across a population with varied anatomical parameters. Input features will be noisy, low-dimensional kinematics and inertial data, mimicking wearable sensors, alongside inverse dynamics-derived joint loads. A dual-encoder neural network architecture will be developed: one temporal encoder (e.g., 1D CNN or LSTM) processes the dynamics, and a second encoder processes static anthropometrics. The fused representation will regress to target parameters (patellar ligament geometry, tibial tuberosity position). The model will be validated against clinical MRI-derived parameters from a cohort of athletes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, imaging-free pipeline for personalizing musculoskeletal models of the knee, specifically the patellofemoral joint. It will advance methods for out-of-lab biomechanical profiling, directly contributing to the fields of injury prevention and rehabilitation in athletes. The resulting technique could be integrated into portable sensing systems for real-time, subject-specific load estimation.
proposals.novelty: While inverse dynamics and parameter estimation exist, combining low-fidelity wearable sensor data with a deep learning framework to infer *personal anatomical geometry* (not just forces) for the patellofemoral joint is novel. This moves beyond generic model scaling to infer parameters traditionally requiring medical imaging.

proposals.title: Clinically-Driven Denoising: A Supervised Learning Framework for MRI Reconstruction Informed by Patellar Instability Outcomes in Athletes
proposals.motivation: Current MRI reconstruction and denoising algorithms are optimized for generic image quality metrics (e.g., PSNR, SSIM), which may not correlate with diagnostic accuracy for specific pathologies. For patellar tracking disorders in overhead athletes, subtle cartilage and subchondral bone features are critical for diagnosis and prognosis, but are often obscured by noise in accelerated scans. A paradigm shift is needed to directly embed clinical outcome relevance into the reconstruction process itself.
proposals.hypothesis: An MRI reconstruction model trained with a loss function that incorporates a clinical outcome predictor (e.g., future patellar instability risk) will produce images that are superior for diagnostic and prognostic tasks compared to models trained solely on pixel-wise fidelity, even if the latter have better traditional image quality scores.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-task, supervised learning framework. A primary network (e.g., a modified U-Net or a deep cascade of CNN denoisers) will perform undersampled k-space reconstruction or image denoising. Its output will feed a secondary, pre-trained, and frozen network that predicts a clinical outcome (e.g., a segmentation-based metric of patellar malalignment or a classifier for injury risk derived from prior work). The training loss will combine a standard fidelity term (L1/L2) with a task-specific term from the clinical network's intermediate feature layers or output, using gradient stopping to ensure the reconstruction network adapts to preserve clinically salient features. We will use a dataset of knee MRIs from athletes with known clinical outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological framework for 'clinical task-driven' medical image reconstruction, moving beyond purely perceptual quality. It will deliver a validated denoising/reconstruction model specifically tuned for assessing patellofemoral joint health in athletes, potentially enabling more accurate diagnosis from faster or noisier scans. The project will also yield insights into which imaging features are most predictive of clinical outcomes.
proposals.novelty: Instead of using clinical outcomes merely as a final evaluation metric, we propose to directly integrate a clinical outcome predictor into the training loop of an image reconstruction algorithm. This inverts the standard validation paradigm, making clinical utility an explicit optimization target rather than a post-hoc assessment.

proposals.title: Adversarially Constrained Multi-task Learning for Diagnostic-Quality Knee MRI Reconstruction and Patellofemoral Segmentation in Athletes
proposals.motivation: In sports medicine, accurate segmentation of patellofemoral structures from MRI is crucial for quantifying injury risk and tracking rehabilitation, but often relies on noisy or low-resolution clinical scans. While multi-task learning can jointly perform reconstruction and segmentation, the segmentation task can corrupt the reconstructed image, removing diagnostically relevant details. This limits the clinical utility of the reconstructed scan for broader diagnostic tasks beyond the specific segmentation target.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-task network with a cyclically consistent and adversarially constrained architecture can produce high-fidelity, diagnostically complete knee MRI reconstructions while simultaneously achieving state-of-the-art segmentation accuracy for patellar cartilage and surrounding tissues.
proposals.method: We will implement a U-Net-based architecture with a shared encoder and two separate decoders for reconstruction and segmentation. A cyclic consistency loss will enforce that segmentations from the reconstructed image match the primary segmentation output. A task-discriminative adversarial loss, using a PatchGAN discriminator, will be applied solely to the reconstruction decoder, training it to produce images indistinguishable from those generated by a baseline denoising autoencoder trained only for reconstruction. The model will be trained and validated on pre-2026 public knee MRI datasets (e.g., OAI, MRNet) and a curated set of athlete scans.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated multi-task framework that provides both a robust segmentation tool for patellofemoral analysis and a high-quality, diagnostically trustworthy image suitable for clinical review. It will provide a methodological blueprint for ensuring task disentanglement in medical image analysis, preserving the general utility of intermediate image outputs.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of a task-specific adversarial loss applied only to the reconstruction decoder, combined with cyclic consistency, directly addresses the problem of task-corrupted reconstructions, a known but under-addressed flaw in standard multi-task learning for medical imaging.

proposals.title: Physics-Informed Multi-Task Learning for Accelerated Patellar MRI Reconstruction and Cartilage Segmentation in Athletes
proposals.motivation: Accelerated MRI is crucial for dynamic joint imaging in athletes, but undersampling introduces noise and artifacts that degrade downstream tasks like cartilage segmentation for injury assessment. Current pipelines treat reconstruction and segmentation as separate, sequential steps, which can propagate errors and obscure subtle pathologies. A unified method that leverages anatomical knowledge to guide reconstruction could improve both image quality and diagnostic utility from limited data.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-task deep learning model, trained jointly for MRI reconstruction and semantic segmentation with a physics-informed data fidelity loss, will produce more anatomically plausible images from undersampled k-space data and more accurate segmentation maps than a sequential approach.
proposals.method: We will develop a U-Net-based architecture with a shared encoder processing undersampled k-space data (via a learned or fixed forward operator) and two decoders: one for image reconstruction using a combined L1/L2 loss with a k-space data consistency term, and one for multi-class segmentation (patellar cartilage, bone, background) using a Dice loss. The joint loss function will weight these terms. The model will be trained and validated on a curated dataset of knee MRI scans from athletes, with simulated undersampling patterns (e.g., Cartesian, radial). Performance will be benchmarked against state-of-the-art sequential methods (e.g., MoDL for reconstruction followed by a separate segmentation network).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, end-to-end framework that provides diagnostically relevant, high-quality images and segmentations from accelerated acquisitions. It will contribute a new benchmark dataset for patellar imaging in athletes and demonstrate the value of anatomical priors for ill-posed MRI reconstruction, potentially reducing scan times for injury screening.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the direct adaptation of the joint inversion-and-segmentation paradigm from geophysical full-waveform inversion to medical MRI, framing segmentation as a physics-informed auxiliary task to constrain reconstruction, which is distinct from prior multi-task medical imaging works that typically share features for related visual tasks.

proposals.title: Joint MRI Reconstruction and Patellofemoral Cartilage Segmentation via Multi-task Learning for Enhanced Analysis of Overhead Athlete Injury Patterns
proposals.motivation: In sports medicine, particularly for overhead athletes, precise quantification of patellofemoral cartilage from MRI is critical for assessing valgus extension overload and injury risk. However, clinical and research MRI scans are often acquired with fast, noisy protocols, where traditional sequential processing (reconstruction then segmentation) can propagate errors and obscure subtle anatomical adaptations. A unified framework that improves both image quality and segmentation accuracy from sub-sampled k-space data is needed for reliable, automated analysis in clinical trials and injury prevention studies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-task deep learning model, trained to jointly optimize for MRI reconstruction and patellar cartilage segmentation, will produce more accurate and clinically relevant segmentations from accelerated acquisitions than a sequential pipeline, by leveraging shared anatomical priors.
proposals.method: We will develop a supervised multi-task network with a shared encoder and two task-specific decoders. The shared encoder will process under-sampled k-space data transformed via a non-uniform FFT layer. One decoder will perform image-domain reconstruction using a U-Net architecture with residual blocks, optimized with a combination of L1 loss and perceptual loss. The parallel decoder will output a pixel-wise segmentation mask for patellar cartilage, using a Dice loss. The joint model will be trained end-to-end on a curated dataset of knee MRIs from athletes, with tasks weighted dynamically via uncertainty weighting. Validation will use data from the IWOAI Knee Segmentation Challenge framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, end-to-end pipeline for producing diagnostic-quality segmentations directly from accelerated MRI data, reducing the need for high-quality, slow scans in longitudinal studies. It will contribute a novel benchmark for joint reconstruction-segmentation in musculoskeletal imaging, with direct application to quantifying anatomical changes in athlete cohorts described in Chaudhari's previous work.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task learning exists, its application to directly couple the inverse problem of MRI reconstruction with the precise segmentation of small, clinically critical structures like patellar cartilage is novel. This approach moves beyond using pre-reconstructed images, explicitly tailoring the reconstruction to enhance a specific downstream quantitative analysis task.

proposals.title: The Multi-Anatomy MRI Data Commons (MAMDC): A Standardized Framework for Vendor-Agnostic k-Space and Labeled Image Data
proposals.motivation: Current MRI research is hampered by fragmented, single-anatomy datasets with inconsistent acquisition protocols and annotation schemas. This fragmentation impedes the development of generalizable deep learning models for reconstruction and analysis, as algorithms trained on one anatomy or scanner type often fail to translate. Inspired by the success of large-scale, standardized surveys in astronomy (e.g., SDSS), a unified, multi-anatomy data commons is needed to catalyze reproducible and robust methodological advances in medical imaging.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that establishing a standardized, vendor-agnostic framework for acquiring raw k-space data and structured pathology labels across multiple anatomies (brain, spine, cardiac) will enable the development of deep learning models with superior generalization and performance compared to models trained on single-anatomy, single-vendor datasets.
proposals.method: We will define a minimal, vendor-neutral acquisition protocol for T1/T2-weighted sequences, capturing raw k-space and DICOM images. A unified annotation framework will be implemented using established ontologies (RadLex, SNOMED CT) for labeling pathologies and anatomical structures. We will initially curate and release a pilot dataset of knee (leveraging existing segmentation challenge data), brain, and cardiac MRI. For algorithm development, we will benchmark state-of-the-art supervised deep learning models (e.g., U-Net variants for segmentation, DAGAN or MoDL for reconstruction) on this commons to assess cross-anatomy generalization.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first open, multi-anatomy MRI dataset with paired raw k-space and standardized labels, serving as a benchmark for generalizable algorithm development. It will provide a concrete framework and pilot data to demonstrate the feasibility and value of large-scale, standardized medical imaging data collection, analogous to astronomical surveys, for accelerating methodological research.
proposals.novelty: While data commons exist (e.g., The Cancer Imaging Archive), none provide standardized, multi-anatomy raw k-space data paired with structured labels. The novelty lies in applying the 'large-scale survey' paradigm from astronomy to medical imaging acquisition and curation, explicitly targeting the development of cross-domain generalizable models rather than single-task solutions.

proposals.title: Multi-Anatomy MRI-KspaceNet: A Curated Dataset of Paired Raw k-Space and Segmentation Labels for Brain, Spine, and Cardiac MRI to Advance Supervised Reconstruction and Denoising
proposals.motivation: Public MRI datasets for deep learning in musculoskeletal imaging, like those from knee segmentation challenges, have accelerated algorithm development for tasks like cartilage quantification. However, similar open-source resources pairing raw, complex-valued k-space data with high-quality anatomical labels for other critical anatomies (brain, spine, cardiac) are scarce. This lack hinders the development and fair benchmarking of supervised deep learning methods for image reconstruction, denoising, and segmentation that operate directly on the raw data domain, limiting their clinical translation beyond the knee.
proposals.hypothesis: By creating and releasing a standardized, multi-anatomy dataset of paired k-space and expert segmentation labels, we will enable the development of more robust, anatomy-specific supervised models for MRI reconstruction and analysis, leading to quantifiable improvements in image quality and segmentation accuracy compared to models trained solely on image-domain data or generic datasets.
proposals.method: We will curate a dataset from existing clinical and public research scans (e.g., from ADNI for brain, ACDC challenge for cardiac), ensuring ethical approvals. For each anatomy, we will reconstruct magnitude images from the raw k-space data using a standard inverse Fourier transform. Expert radiologists will then provide manual or semi-automated segmentations of key structures (e.g., brain tissues, vertebral bodies, cardiac chambers). The core technical contribution is the systematic pairing and formatting of these elements. We will benchmark the dataset using a U-Net variant adapted for complex-valued k-space input, training separate models for each anatomy to perform joint denoising and segmentation, comparing performance against image-domain baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first open-access, multi-anatomy resource of paired k-space and labels, directly supporting research in supervised k-space learning. It will establish a benchmark for evaluating reconstruction-segmentation models across anatomies. The dataset will lower the barrier to entry for developing clinically relevant MRI methods, extending the success seen in knee MRI to neurology, orthopedics, and cardiology.
proposals.novelty: While knee-specific k-space datasets exist, a curated multi-anatomy collection with expert labels is novel. The proposal uniquely focuses on enabling direct, supervised learning in k-space for diverse clinical targets, moving beyond single-anatomy or image-domain transfer learning approaches common pre-2026.

proposals.title: Enhanced Sensitivity in Longitudinal Cartilage Thickness Mapping for Osteoarthritis: A Supervised Deep Learning Super-Resolution Approach
proposals.motivation: Longitudinal MRI studies of knee osteoarthritis (OA) progression are limited by the difficulty in detecting subtle, early cartilage changes due to image noise, partial volume effects, and typical clinical scan resolutions (e.g., 1-1.5mm slice thickness). This reduces statistical power in clinical trials and delays the assessment of disease-modifying therapies. Super-resolution (SR) techniques, which reconstruct high-resolution images from lower-resolution acquisitions, offer a potential solution but have not been rigorously validated for quantifying temporal change in clinical OA studies.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a deep learning-based super-resolution model to sequential knee MRI scans will improve the sensitivity and statistical power for detecting longitudinal changes in cartilage thickness compared to analyses performed on native-resolution images.
proposals.method: We will develop a supervised, 3D convolutional neural network (CNN) for SR, likely based on an Enhanced Deep Super-Resolution (EDSR) or Residual Dense Network (RDN) architecture, tailored for multi-contrast knee MRI (e.g., DESS, IW TSE). The model will be trained on paired low-resolution (simulated via downsampling) and high-resolution scans from public datasets like the Osteoarthritis Initiative (OAI). We will then apply the trained model to longitudinal OAI timepoints, perform automated cartilage segmentation using a pre-existing U-Net-based method (leveraging Chaudhari's prior work), and compute regional thickness maps. Sensitivity to change will be evaluated by comparing the effect sizes (e.g., standardized response mean) of thickness change between SR-processed and native-resolution images over 12-24 month intervals.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct evidence for the utility of SR in enhancing sensitivity to longitudinal cartilage change, a critical biomarker for OA. It will deliver a validated, open-source processing pipeline for SR-enhanced quantitative cartilage analysis, potentially enabling shorter or smaller clinical trials for OA therapeutics. The findings will bridge the gap between computational image enhancement and practical clinical research methodology.
proposals.novelty: While SR has been applied to single-timepoint knee MRI for segmentation improvement, its specific value for increasing statistical power in longitudinal progression studies remains an unvalidated and non-obvious application. This proposal uniquely frames SR not as a pure image enhancement tool, but as a method to improve the effect size of a key quantitative biomarker in longitudinal analysis.

proposals.title: The Impact of Deep Learning-Based Super-Resolution on the Accuracy of Automated Patellofemoral Cartilage Segmentation in Athlete Knee MRI
proposals.motivation: Accurate quantification of patellar and femoral cartilage from MRI is critical for assessing injury risk and progression in overhead athletes, as highlighted by Chaudhari's prior work on valgus extension overload. However, clinical and portable imaging often involves trade-offs between resolution, scan time, and signal-to-noise ratio, which can degrade the performance of automated segmentation algorithms essential for efficient analysis in trials and rehabilitation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying a deep learning-based super-resolution (SR) preprocessing step to clinical-resolution knee MRI will significantly improve the accuracy and robustness of subsequent supervised deep learning models for cartilage and tissue segmentation, particularly in regions prone to overload injuries.
proposals.method: We will curate a dataset of paired high-resolution (HR) and simulated low-resolution (LR) knee MRI scans from athletes. A state-of-the-art SR network, such as Enhanced Deep Super-Resolution (EDSR) or a Residual Channel Attention Network (RCAN), will be trained to map LR to HR images. The impact will be evaluated by training and testing a U-Net or nnU-Net segmentation model under three conditions: on native LR images, on SR-processed images, and on native HR images (gold standard). Segmentation accuracy will be assessed using Dice similarity coefficient (DSC) and surface distance metrics, specifically in the patellofemoral compartment.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative evidence on the value of SR as a preprocessing tool for musculoskeletal tissue segmentation in athletes, directly informing the design of automated analysis pipelines for clinical trials and injury prevention studies. It will establish best practices for handling image quality variability in real-world sports medicine imaging.
proposals.novelty: While SR and segmentation are both active fields, their synergistic application for sports injury-related tissue quantification is unexplored. The novelty lies in rigorously testing this pipeline specifically for patellofemoral anatomy in athlete cohorts, linking computational image enhancement directly to a clinically relevant segmentation task for injury mechanics.

proposals.title: Physics-Informed Super-Resolution for Patellar MRI: A Simulation-Based Training Framework for Enhanced Cartilage and Injury Assessment
proposals.motivation: High-resolution MRI is critical for assessing patellar cartilage morphology and subtle injury patterns in athletes, but acquisition is slow and prone to motion artifacts. Super-resolution (SR) deep learning offers a solution but is bottlenecked by the scarcity of perfectly aligned high- and low-resolution clinical image pairs for training. This limits the robustness and generalizability of SR models in clinical practice, particularly for musculoskeletal applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a supervised SR network on a large, diverse dataset of synthetic MRI pairs generated by a physics-based simulator will yield a model that generalizes more effectively to real clinical knee MRI scans, outperforming models trained solely on limited real data.
proposals.method: We will implement a modular pipeline using the open-source MRI simulator SIMRI or a Bloch-equation-based simulator to generate synthetic, multi-contrast (e.g., T1, T2, PD) knee MRI volumes. Realistic coil sensitivity, noise, and partial voluming will be modeled. A large dataset of synthetic high-resolution images and their corresponding downsampled, noisy low-resolution counterparts will be created. This dataset will be used to train a state-of-the-art SR network, such as a Residual Dense Network (RDN) or Enhanced Deep Super-Resolution (EDSR) network, in a supervised manner. The pre-trained model will then be fine-tuned and evaluated on a limited set of real patient scans from cohorts of overhead athletes, focusing on patellar cartilage segmentation accuracy and image quality metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, data-efficient framework for developing SR models in musculoskeletal radiology, reducing dependency on scarce clinical paired data. It will deliver a validated SR tool for enhancing patellar imaging, potentially improving the quantification of cartilage thickness and the detection of early degenerative or overload changes in athletic populations.
proposals.novelty: While simulation has been explored for MRI reconstruction, its targeted application to generate training data for super-resolution networks in musculoskeletal imaging, specifically for the patellofemoral joint, is novel. This approach uniquely integrates physics-based forward modeling with modern supervised deep learning for SR, bridging a critical gap in data availability.

proposals.title: Self-Supervised Super-Resolution for Patellar Imaging Using Paired Low-Resolution MRI Scans
proposals.motivation: High-resolution MRI is critical for detailed assessment of patellar cartilage and injury patterns in athletes, but acquiring such data is time-consuming and costly. Supervised deep learning for super-resolution requires scarce, perfectly aligned high-resolution ground truth scans, limiting its clinical applicability. A method that learns from readily available, variable-quality low-resolution scans would democratize high-quality imaging for injury prevention and rehabilitation studies.
proposals.hypothesis: A deep learning model can learn a robust super-resolution mapping for musculoskeletal MRI by exploiting the inherent information redundancy across multiple, differently degraded low-resolution scans of the same anatomical region, without ever seeing a true high-resolution target.
proposals.method: We will adapt a cycle-consistent generative adversarial network (CycleGAN) framework, but instead of style transfer, the cycles will enforce consistency between different low-resolution (LR) acquisition paths. Pairs of LR patellar scans (e.g., from different clinical protocols or simulated downsamplings) of the same subject will serve as input. The generator will learn to map from one LR domain to a latent high-resolution space and back to the other LR domain, with discriminators ensuring realism. Specific architectures like U-Net generators and PatchGAN discriminators will be used, trained on datasets from prior segmentation challenges and clinical studies of overhead athletes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical pipeline for enhancing the resolution of clinical knee MRI without the need for curated high-resolution training data. It will directly benefit quantitative cartilage analysis and injury pattern detection in athletic populations. The method will be a step towards portable, high-quality imaging in out-of-lab rehabilitation settings.
proposals.novelty: While unsupervised domain translation exists, applying the cycle-consistency principle specifically to learn a super-resolution function from dual low-resolution inputs, rather than for style or contrast transfer, is a novel approach for medical imaging. This bypasses the fundamental bottleneck of requiring high-resolution ground truth, which is a key limitation of pre-2026 supervised super-resolution methods.

proposals.title: Clinical Validation of Deep Learning Super-Resolution for Enhanced Detection of Patellar Cartilage Lesions in Overhead Athletes
proposals.motivation: In sports medicine, early detection of subtle patellar cartilage pathology, such as in valgus extension overload, is critical for preventing long-term joint degeneration. However, clinical MRI protocols face a trade-off between resolution, scan time, and signal-to-noise ratio, potentially obscuring early degenerative changes. Super-resolution (SR) methods promise to enhance image detail from standard acquisitions, but their diagnostic utility for specific clinical tasks remains unproven.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a deep learning-based super-resolution network, trained in a supervised manner, can generate high-resolution knee MRI images from standard clinical sequences that improve radiologists' diagnostic confidence and accuracy for detecting early patellar cartilage lesions compared to native-resolution images.
proposals.method: We will adapt a state-of-the-art supervised SR architecture, such as Enhanced Deep Residual Networks for Super-Resolution (EDSR) or a Residual Channel Attention Network (RCAN), trained on paired low-resolution and high-resolution knee MRI patches. The model will be trained and validated using a curated dataset from overhead athletes, with segmentation masks (from prior work) used to focus loss functions on cartilage regions. The clinical validation will involve a reader study where radiologists assess native and SR-reconstructed images for specific patellar cartilage pathologies, using arthroscopic findings or expert consensus as the reference standard.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first task-specific clinical validation of SR for patellar imaging, moving beyond technical metrics like PSNR. It will establish whether SR can translate to tangible diagnostic benefits in a high-impact sports medicine context, informing future clinical adoption. The study will also produce a publicly available benchmark dataset of paired images with expert annotations for pathology.
proposals.novelty: While SR has been applied to knee MRI, its validation has focused on general image quality or segmentation accuracy. This proposal uniquely targets a specific, clinically relevant diagnostic task (patellar lesion detection in a defined athletic population) and evaluates utility through a blinded reader study, a crucial step for clinical translation that is largely missing from pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Spatio-Temporal Deep Learning Framework for Super-Resolution of Dynamic Knee MRI Sequences in Athletic Joint Motion Analysis
proposals.motivation: Dynamic MRI is crucial for assessing patellofemoral kinematics and soft-tissue injuries in athletes, but its clinical adoption is limited by a fundamental trade-off between spatial resolution, temporal resolution, and scan time. Low-resolution dynamic sequences obscure critical anatomical details needed for precise tracking of patellar motion and cartilage contact patterns, hindering the quantitative analysis of valgus extension overload and other dynamic pathologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a supervised deep learning model, trained on paired low-resolution dynamic and high-resolution static knee MRI scans, can generate super-resolved dynamic sequences that preserve accurate temporal motion while enhancing spatial detail for patellar and cartilage structures.
proposals.method: We will extend a proven 2D super-resolution CNN (e.g., SRCNN, EDSR) into a 3D spatio-temporal architecture. The model will be trained using a loss function combining pixel-wise MSE with a temporal consistency loss (e.g., using 3D convolutional gradients) and a perceptual loss based on a pre-trained VGG network. Training data will be synthesized from high-resolution 3D knee MRI scans (e.g., from the IWOAI challenge) by simulating down-sampled dynamic sequences across plausible motion paths, informed by the biomechanical patterns identified in Chaudhari's prior work on throwing athletes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational tool to enhance the diagnostic utility of rapid, low-resolution dynamic MRI, enabling more precise quantification of patellar tracking and cartilage deformation during motion. It directly supports the translation of portable sensing and injury prevention research into the MRI domain by offering a high-fidelity imaging method for validating out-of-lab biomechanical models.
proposals.novelty: While single-image super-resolution for static knee MRI is established, applying it to dynamic sequences while enforcing temporal coherence for joint kinematics is a novel challenge. Our approach uniquely integrates biomechanically informed motion simulation for training data generation with a spatio-temporal loss function tailored for articular cartilage and patellar imaging.

proposals.title: A Unified, Deep Learning-Based Pipeline for Rapid MRI Acquisition and Automated Cartilage Quantification in Patellofemoral Osteoarthritis
proposals.motivation: Current clinical workflows for knee osteoarthritis (OA) assessment are fragmented, involving separate steps for MRI acquisition, often with long scan times, and subsequent manual or semi-automated image analysis. This inefficiency hinders rapid, quantitative evaluation in both clinical trials and point-of-care settings, particularly for tracking subtle patellofemoral changes in athletes at risk of OA. An integrated system that accelerates acquisition and provides immediate, objective metrics is critically needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a rapid, optimized MRI pulse sequence with a supervised deep learning model for simultaneous denoising and segmentation will yield a clinically viable, end-to-end pipeline that provides accurate cartilage thickness and volume measurements from the patellofemoral joint in under 10 minutes total scan-and-analysis time.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage pipeline. First, we will implement and optimize a rapid 3D double-echo steady-state (DESS) or similar fast MRI sequence for knee imaging, prioritizing the patellofemoral compartment. Second, we will train a cascaded convolutional neural network (CNN), building on architectures like U-Net or nnU-Net, using a dataset of knee MRIs from prior studies (e.g., the IWOAI challenge).
