The network will perform joint image denoising and semantic segmentation of patellar and femoral trochlear cartilage. The pipeline will be validated on a cohort of overhead throwing athletes, correlating automated metrics with clinical pain scores and valgus overload indicators.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a fully operational, open-source software/hardware pipeline for rapid OA assessment, directly translating research in accelerated MRI and supervised segmentation to the clinic. It will provide a new tool for longitudinal monitoring of cartilage in at-risk athletic populations, enabling studies on the early biomechanical origins of patellofemoral OA. The integrated design moves beyond standalone analysis tools toward a holistic diagnostic system.
proposals.novelty: While accelerated MRI and automated segmentation exist separately, their tight integration into a single, optimized workflow for the patellofemoral joint is novel. The proposed method uniquely addresses the specific need for speed and automation in athletic cohorts, combining Dr. Chaudhari's expertise in denoising, segmentation, and injury patterns into a unified solution.

proposals.title: Consensus-Driven Standardization of Quantitative MRI Biomarkers for Knee Osteoarthritis: A Framework for Automated Segmentation Tool Validation
proposals.motivation: Automated segmentation tools for knee MRI, like those from the IWOAI challenge, are proliferating in research and clinical trials for osteoarthritis (OA). However, there is no consensus on which quantitative biomarkers (e.g., cartilage thickness, volume, T2/T1ρ relaxation times) should be reported or how they should be computed from segmentations, leading to incomparable results across studies and hindering clinical translation. This lack of standardization undermines the utility of these powerful tools for longitudinal monitoring and drug efficacy trials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Delphi consensus process involving musculoskeletal radiologists, osteoarthritis researchers, and algorithm developers can establish a core set of standardized, clinically relevant quantitative metrics and computational methods for automated knee OA analysis, thereby improving reproducibility and cross-study comparability.
proposals.method: We will first conduct a systematic literature review (pre-2026) to catalog all quantitative metrics reported in studies using automated knee MRI segmentation. Using a modified Delphi method, an international panel of experts will iteratively rate and refine these metrics to establish a core standardized set. For each consensus metric, we will implement and compare computation methods using outputs from state-of-the-art supervised deep learning segmentation models (e.g., nnU-Net variants) trained on public datasets like the Osteoarthritis Initiative (OAI). The final framework will include specific definitions, formulas, and preferred reporting units.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first expert-validated framework for standardizing quantitative biomarker reporting from automated knee OA tools, directly addressing a critical barrier in the field. It will enable more reliable comparison of results across clinical studies and accelerate the validation of imaging biomarkers as endpoints for OA trials, building directly on Dr. Chaudhari's expertise in segmentation, denoising, and clinical imaging methods.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on improving segmentation accuracy (e.g., IWOAI challenge), this proposal uniquely targets the post-segmentation analysis pipeline, applying a formal consensus methodology to standardize the quantitative output—a crucial but overlooked step for clinical translation. It bridges the gap between algorithmic development and clinical application.

proposals.title: Accelerating Dynamic Knee MRI for Patellar Tracking Using Motion-Compensated Video Compression Algorithms
proposals.motivation: Dynamic MRI is crucial for assessing patellar kinematics and soft-tissue injuries in athletes, but its clinical adoption is limited by long acquisition times. Current acceleration methods like compressed sensing (CS) often treat temporal frames independently, failing to fully exploit the high temporal correlation inherent in joint motion sequences. A method that leverages this correlation could significantly reduce scan time without compromising diagnostic quality for patellar tracking studies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying motion estimation and compensation techniques from video coding standards (e.g., HEVC/H.265) to the k-space data of dynamic knee MRI sequences will enable superior spatiotemporal sparsity, allowing for higher acceleration factors in CS reconstruction compared to conventional temporal regularization.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel reconstruction pipeline for highly-undersampled dynamic knee MRI. First, an initial image series will be reconstructed using a conventional CS method. A convolutional neural network (CNN), pre-trained on optical flow estimation, will then extract motion vector fields between consecutive temporal frames. These motion fields will be used to build a motion-compensated temporal prediction model, forming the basis for a novel spatiotemporal regularization term within a supervised learning-based reconstruction framework (e.g., a modified U-Net). The model will be trained and validated on a dataset of fully-sampled dynamic knee MRI scans from athletes with and without patellar instability.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, physics-informed deep learning method for dynamic MRI acceleration that explicitly models inter-frame motion. It will provide a validated framework for faster, high-quality patellar kinematic assessments, directly supporting injury prevention and rehabilitation research in athletes. The project will also demonstrate the tangible benefits of cross-pollinating video processing and medical imaging techniques.
proposals.novelty: While CS and deep learning are established for MRI acceleration, the direct integration of video compression's core motion prediction mechanisms into the MRI reconstruction optimization loop is novel. This approach specifically targets the redundancy in *moving anatomical structures*, unlike generic temporal models, making it uniquely suited for joint kinematics.

proposals.title: Few-Shot Meta-Learning for Label-Efficient Segmentation of Knee Tissues in MRI
proposals.motivation: Accurate segmentation of knee tissues (cartilage, meniscus, bone) from MRI is critical for quantifying injury and tracking rehabilitation in athletes, but manual labeling is expensive and expertise-intensive. Current deep learning methods require large, fully annotated datasets, which are scarce for many pathologies and imaging protocols. Adapting few-shot learning from natural images could enable robust segmentation models from only a handful of labeled examples, accelerating research and clinical application.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-learning framework, pre-trained on a diverse set of natural image segmentation tasks, can be effectively adapted to segment knee MRI tissues with high accuracy using only a few annotated slices per patient.
proposals.method: We will adapt a prototypical network or a relation network architecture, pre-trained on the PASCAL-5i few-shot segmentation benchmark. The model will be fine-tuned using a meta-learning strategy on a small, curated dataset of knee MRIs (e.g., from the Osteoarthritis Initiative). The support set will contain few-shot examples (e.g., 1-5 slices) of target tissues, and the query set will be used to evaluate segmentation performance on unseen slices. We will compare against fully supervised U-Net baselines trained with limited data.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical, label-efficient segmentation tool for knee MRI, directly applicable to clinical studies of patellar injuries and osteoarthritis. It will provide a methodological framework for adapting computer vision few-shot techniques to medical imaging, reducing the annotation burden. The results will advance personalized sports medicine by enabling rapid model adaptation for individual athlete monitoring.
proposals.novelty: While few-shot learning is established in computer vision, its application to knee tissue segmentation is novel, particularly the strategic transfer of knowledge from natural image domains to medical imaging via meta-learning. This approach moves beyond simple fine-tuning of supervised models or using synthetic data.

proposals.title: Active Learning for Efficient Expert Annotation in Knee MRI Segmentation: A Strategy for Patellar and Cartilage Analysis in Athletic Populations
proposals.motivation: Manual segmentation of knee structures (e.g., patellar cartilage, menisci) from MRI is essential for quantifying injury and adaptation in athletes but is prohibitively time-consuming for large-scale studies. Current supervised deep learning models require vast, fully annotated datasets, creating a major bottleneck in radiology and sports medicine research. An intelligent method to minimize expert annotation effort while maintaining segmentation accuracy would accelerate the development of robust quantitative imaging biomarkers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an active learning framework, which iteratively selects the most informative knee MRI slices for expert annotation based on model uncertainty and diversity, can achieve state-of-the-art segmentation performance for patellar and articular cartilage using a fraction of the fully annotated data required by standard supervised methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline using a 3D U-Net as a base segmentation model for knee MRI volumes. An active learning loop will be implemented where, after initial training on a small seed set, the model queries a large unlabeled pool. We will test query strategies combining predictive uncertainty (e.g., Monte Carlo Dropout or entropy) and feature-space diversity (using embeddings from the penultimate layer) to select the most valuable slices for expert annotation. The process iterates until performance plateaus, evaluated on a held-out test set from a dataset of overhead throwing athletes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, practical framework for reducing the annotation burden in medical image segmentation, specifically for knee MRI in athletic populations. It will deliver a publicly available codebase and a detailed analysis of query strategy efficacy, directly supporting future clinical trials and longitudinal studies of knee injury highlighted in Dr. Chaudhari's prior scoping review.
proposals.novelty: While active learning is known in computer vision, its tailored application to knee MRI, with a focus on selecting individual 2D slices within 3D volumes for patellar and cartilage segmentation in a specific athletic cohort, represents a novel and clinically impactful optimization. This bridges Dr. Chaudhari's expertise in knee imaging, segmentation challenges, and supervised learning with an efficient annotation methodology.

proposals.title: A Cloud-Based, Federated Learning Network for Real-Time, Multi-Center Analysis of Knee MRI in Athletic Populations
proposals.motivation: Current knee MRI analysis for injury prevention and rehabilitation, especially in athletes, is hampered by single-center datasets and manual, time-consuming quantification. This limits the generalizability of models and delays clinical insights. A scalable, real-time analysis network is needed to aggregate diverse data for robust, population-wide assessment of patellar alignment, cartilage health, and injury risk.
proposals.hypothesis: A cloud-based federated learning network, integrating advanced denoising and segmentation models, can enable accurate, real-time, and privacy-preserving analysis of knee MRI across multiple institutions, leading to more consistent quantification of patellofemoral anatomy and injury patterns in athletic cohorts.
proposals.method: We will develop a centralized cloud platform for DICOM ingestion and real-time inference using pre-trained, supervised deep learning models (e.g., nnU-Net variants) for patellar and cartilage segmentation. To enable multi-center training without data sharing, we will implement a federated learning framework (e.g., using PySyft or TensorFlow Federated) where local models are trained on each institution's data and only model weights are aggregated. Domain adaptation techniques will be applied to harmonize features across different MRI scanners and protocols.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a functional prototype for scalable, multi-center knee MRI analysis, directly addressing the need for large-scale data in sports medicine research. It will provide validated benchmarks for real-time anatomical quantification and demonstrate a practical federated learning pipeline for radiology, enhancing model generalizability while preserving data privacy.
proposals.novelty: While cloud-based analysis exists, integrating a federated learning approach specifically for athletic knee MRI with a focus on patellofemoral and cartilage metrics is novel. This moves beyond single-center challenges towards a live, privacy-conscious network for continuous model improvement and immediate clinical feedback.

proposals.title: Super-Resolution-Guided Segmentation for Patellar Cartilage Quantification from Fast 2D MRI Sequences
proposals.motivation: High-resolution 3D MRI sequences are the gold standard for automated cartilage segmentation but are time-consuming, expensive, and less accessible in routine clinical or sports medicine workflows. Faster 2D or low-resolution 3D sequences are commonly acquired but considered suboptimal for quantitative analysis, creating a bottleneck for widespread adoption of automated methods in injury prevention and rehabilitation monitoring, as highlighted in Dr. Chaudhari's recent scoping review.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a deep learning model integrating a lightweight super-resolution network with a segmentation network can achieve clinically acceptable patellar cartilage segmentation accuracy from fast, low-resolution 2D MRI sequences, performing comparably to models trained on high-resolution 3D data.
proposals.method: We will develop a dual-task convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture. The first module will be a residual channel attention network (RCAN) for single-image super-resolution, trained to upsample 2D fast spin-echo (FSE) sequences. This will be jointly optimized with a U-Net variant (e.g., Attention U-Net) for segmentation, using a multi-task loss function. The model will be trained and validated on a dataset pairing low-resolution 2D and high-resolution 3D knee MRI scans from athletes, leveraging transfer learning from the 2019 IWOAI segmentation challenge models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated method for accurate cartilage quantification from rapid MRI protocols, directly enhancing clinical workflow efficiency. It will contribute a new benchmark dataset pairing sequence types and an open-source model to bridge the gap between research-grade segmentation and practical, point-of-care musculoskeletal imaging.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work focusing on denoising or segmenting high-quality inputs, this approach explicitly challenges the necessity of high-resolution 3D data by jointly learning to enhance and segment from inherently noisy, low-resolution 2D clinical scans, a non-trivial problem given the loss of through-plane information.

proposals.title: Beyond Dice: Clinically-Informed Segmentation Metrics for Patellofemoral Joint Analysis in Overhead Athletes
proposals.motivation: In musculoskeletal imaging, segmentation models are evaluated primarily by geometric overlap metrics like Dice. However, for clinical tasks such as assessing patellar instability or cartilage wear in athletes, the clinical relevance of a segmentation error depends on its anatomical location and impact on derived biomarkers. A high Dice score may mask errors in clinically critical regions, misleading model validation for diagnostic or prognostic use.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that segmentation metrics weighted by clinical importance—such as proximity to load-bearing cartilage surfaces or anatomical landmarks tied to injury patterns—will correlate more strongly with the accuracy of downstream clinical measurements (e.g., cartilage thickness maps, trochlear geometry) than standard Dice.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework to compute spatially-weighted segmentation metrics. First, using expert-annotated MRI from overhead athletes, we will define clinical importance maps for patellar and femoral cartilage, emphasizing subregions linked to valgus overload. We will then train a U-Net variant with a loss function incorporating these maps. New metrics will be defined, such as 'Clinical Surface Dice' (focusing on articular surfaces) and 'Landmark Distance Error'. Their correlation with errors in key clinical parameters (e.g., patellar tilt, cartilage volume in specific zones) will be statistically tested against Dice.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated set of segmentation evaluation metrics that bridge the gap between technical performance and clinical utility. It will deliver a framework for creating task-specific evaluation standards in musculoskeletal radiology, directly supporting more reliable deployment of AI tools in sports medicine and clinical trials for cartilage quantification.
proposals.novelty: While the limitation of Dice is acknowledged, prior work has not systematically defined and validated anatomy-specific, clinically-weighted metrics for patellofemoral analysis, nor explicitly tied them to athlete-specific injury patterns and prognostic endpoints derived from segmentations.

proposals.title: The Multi-Tissue Knee Segmentation (MTKS) Challenge: A Benchmark for Automated Quantification of Bones, Ligaments, and Osteophytes in MRI
proposals.motivation: Current knee MRI segmentation challenges, like the 2019 IWOAI, focus primarily on cartilage, limiting their clinical utility for comprehensive joint assessment. Pathologies such as valgus extension overload in athletes involve complex interactions between multiple tissues, including bone adaptation, ligament integrity, and osteophyte formation. A standardized, multi-institute benchmark for segmenting these structures is critically needed to advance automated methods for holistic injury analysis and osteoarthritis progression monitoring.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-task, deep learning framework trained on a diverse, multi-institutional dataset can achieve clinically acceptable segmentation accuracy for bones, ligaments, and osteophytes simultaneously, outperforming single-task models and providing more comprehensive biomarkers for pathology.
proposals.method: We will curate a multi-institutional dataset of knee MRIs with expert manual annotations for femoral/tibial bones, the ACL/PCL, and osteophytes. The core technical approach will be a supervised, multi-decoder U-Net variant (e.g., nnU-Net or a Attention U-Net) with a shared encoder. We will implement specific preprocessing for ligament contrast and employ a combined loss function (Dice + Focal Loss) to handle the severe class imbalance posed by small structures like ligaments and osteophytes. Performance will be benchmarked against established single-structure baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first public benchmark dataset and challenge for multi-tissue knee segmentation, directly enabling more comprehensive automated analysis of sports injuries and osteoarthritis. It will yield a robust, open-source model that quantifies interrelated pathological features, moving beyond isolated cartilage assessment to support clinical trials and biomechanical studies requiring full-joint metrics.
proposals.novelty: While prior challenges focused on cartilage or bone, this integrates the segmentation of mechanically crucial ligaments and pathognomonic osteophytes into a single benchmark, addressing the multi-tissue reality of knee pathology. The explicit inclusion of osteophytes, which are irregular and challenging, within a multi-task learning framework for sports medicine applications is novel.

proposals.title: A Framework for Longitudinal Consistency Metrics (LCM): Benchmarking Segmentation Stability in Serial Knee MRI for Patellofemoral Joint Analysis
proposals.motivation: In sports medicine and orthopedics, longitudinal MRI analysis is critical for tracking patellar cartilage health, injury progression, and rehabilitation in athletes. However, current segmentation evaluation metrics (e.g., Dice, Hausdorff distance) assess single-time-point accuracy, not consistency across serial scans. This gap hinders the reliable quantification of subtle anatomical changes, which is essential for clinical trials and personalized injury prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a novel set of longitudinal consistency metrics (LCMs), which explicitly penalize biologically implausible segmentation fluctuations between time points, will provide a more reliable and clinically relevant benchmark for evaluating segmentation algorithms in serial imaging studies than traditional static metrics.
proposals.method: We will curate a longitudinal dataset of knee MRI series from overhead throwing athletes, with manual segmentations of patellar cartilage at multiple time points. We will define and implement LCMs, including a temporal Dice similarity coefficient (tDSC) that measures overlap stability, a boundary drift metric, and a volume change plausibility score based on known biological rates. We will benchmark state-of-the-art supervised segmentation models (e.g., nnU-Net variants) and denoising pre-processing methods using both traditional and proposed LCMs, analyzing their correlation with expert-rated segmentation quality and known clinical outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first standardized benchmark for evaluating segmentation consistency in longitudinal musculoskeletal imaging. It will deliver a publicly available metric suite and baseline results, enabling more robust tracking of patellofemoral joint adaptations in athletes. This directly advances the field towards reliable, automated cartilage quantification for clinical trials and longitudinal research.
proposals.novelty: While segmentation challenges (e.g., the IWOAI Knee Segmentation Challenge) focus on single-image accuracy, this proposal shifts the paradigm to temporal consistency. It introduces metrics specifically designed for the longitudinal setting, a critical but unaddressed need in quantitative imaging for injury monitoring and prevention.

proposals.title: Improving Patellar Cartilage Segmentation Robustness in MRI via Aggressive Test-Time Augmentation and Fusion
proposals.motivation: Accurate, automated segmentation of patellar cartilage from MRI is critical for quantifying injury patterns in overhead athletes and for longitudinal clinical trials, as seen in Dr. Chaudhari's prior work. However, single-model deep learning segmentations, especially from limited training data, can be brittle to variations in MRI acquisition parameters, patient positioning, and anatomical presentation. This lack of robustness hinders reliable deployment in clinical and sports medicine settings where consistency is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying aggressive, multi-scale test-time augmentation (TTA) with learned fusion strategies, adapted from successful techniques in natural image segmentation, will significantly improve the robustness and accuracy of a single U-Net model for patellar cartilage segmentation in MRI, outperforming standard single-inference and simple averaging approaches.
proposals.method: We will train a 2D or 3D U-Net (nnU-Net framework) on existing knee MRI datasets (e.g., from the IWOAI challenge). At test time, we will generate multiple predictions per scan using an aggressive augmentation suite including heavy rotations (±30°), scaling (0.7-1.3), intensity shifts, and elastic deformations. Instead of simple averaging, we will implement and compare fusion methods: 1) a learned meta-learner (small CNN) to weight predictions, and 2) an entropy-based uncertainty weighting scheme. Performance will be evaluated on a held-out test set with added synthetic noise and multi-center data to assess robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, practical method to boost the clinical reliability of single-model segmentation without the computational cost of ensemble training. It will deliver a specific pipeline for robust patellar cartilage quantification, directly applicable to studies of valgus extension overload and portable sensing validation. The findings will establish guidelines for effective TTA strategies in musculoskeletal MRI analysis.
proposals.novelty: While TTA is known, its application in musculoskeletal MRI segmentation has been limited to mild augmentations (flips). The novelty lies in systematically translating and evaluating 'aggressive' TTA paradigms from natural images to a clinically specific, small-structure segmentation task, coupled with advanced fusion techniques beyond averaging to optimize the trade-off between augmentation diversity and prediction stability.

proposals.title: Real-Time, Scanner-Integrated Patellofemoral Cartilage Segmentation for Adaptive MRI Protocol Guidance in Athletes
proposals.motivation: Current MRI analysis, including segmentation for cartilage quantification, occurs post-acquisition, creating a delay that prevents intra-scan adaptation. For athletes with complex patellofemoral pain or overload injuries, this means scans may miss critical pathology if the initial protocol is suboptimal. Real-time segmentation during acquisition could enable adaptive imaging, dynamically focusing on regions of interest like the patellar cartilage to improve diagnostic yield and efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a lightweight, supervised deep learning model, optimized for computational efficiency and integrated with the MRI scanner's reconstruction pipeline, can perform accurate segmentation of patellofemoral cartilage from partial k-space data in near real-time, enabling immediate feedback for protocol adjustment.
proposals.method: We will develop a method based on knowledge distillation and model compression. A high-accuracy teacher model (e.g., a 3D U-Net variant) will be trained on fully-sampled, segmented knee MRI datasets from projects like the IWOAI challenge. A compact student model (e.g., a MobileNetV3-inspired 3D CNN) will then be trained to mimic the teacher's predictions using progressively undersampled k-space data simulated via the scanner's API. We will implement inference on the scanner's reconstruction computer using TensorFlow Lite or ONNX Runtime, focusing on latency optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, deployable framework for real-time tissue segmentation within the MRI scanner environment, moving beyond offline analysis. It will provide a concrete technical pathway for adaptive MRI protocols, potentially reducing scan time and improving lesion detection in dynamic joint studies. The optimized model will serve as a foundational tool for real-time biofeedback in rehabilitation and injury prevention research.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on offline segmentation accuracy, the novelty lies in the tight integration of a latency-optimized model into the live data stream of the MRI scanner for the specific purpose of guiding acquisition, a step beyond portable sensing or retrospective analysis.

proposals.title: Predicting Patient-Reported Knee Pain from Automated Cartilage and Meniscus Morphology Features: A Supervised Learning Approach
proposals.motivation: Knee osteoarthritis (OA) pain is a leading cause of disability, yet the link between structural changes seen on MRI and patient-reported symptoms remains poorly quantified. Current clinical assessment relies on semi-quantitative scoring, which is time-consuming and subjective. An automated, quantitative method to predict pain from imaging could improve patient stratification, track disease progression, and serve as a novel imaging biomarker for clinical trials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a supervised learning model, trained on quantitative morphological features extracted from automated knee cartilage and meniscus segmentations, can accurately predict patient-reported pain scores, and that these features will provide superior predictive value compared to traditional clinical radiological assessments.
proposals.method: We will leverage a dataset of knee MRI scans with corresponding patient-reported outcome measures (e.g., KOOS Pain subscale). Using a pre-trained, state-of-the-art deep learning segmentation network (e.g., a nnU-Net variant) from our prior work, we will generate automated segmentations of femoral, tibial, and patellar cartilage, and the menisci. From these segmentations, we will extract a comprehensive set of 3D morphological features (volume, thickness, surface curvature, lesion size/location). These features will be used to train and validate a supervised regression model (e.g., Gradient Boosting Regressor or a shallow neural network) to predict the continuous pain score. Model performance will be evaluated via cross-validation and compared to a baseline model using only demographic and clinical variables.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, quantitative link between automated imaging biomarkers and patient symptomatology in knee OA. It will deliver a proof-of-concept pipeline for 'imaging-to-pain' prediction, moving beyond descriptive segmentation towards functional interpretation. The resulting model could inform personalized treatment plans and provide an objective tool for evaluating therapeutic efficacy in clinical studies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on segmenting structures or correlating single metrics with disease severity, this study frames pain prediction as a supervised inverse problem using a rich, multi-structure feature set derived entirely from automated, high-precision segmentations, integrating Dr. Chaudhari's expertise in denoising, segmentation, and athlete imaging into a novel clinical prediction task.

proposals.title: Quantitative Recovery Assessment of Patellar Cartilage Microstructure in Overhead Athletes Using Supervised Deep Learning for MRI Denoising and Feature-Specific Segmentation
proposals.motivation: Current quantitative MRI metrics for tracking knee recovery, such as bulk cartilage volume or T2 relaxation times, often lack the sensitivity to capture the restoration of small, clinically crucial features like surface fibrillations, fissures, or localized signal changes in the patellar cartilage. This is a critical gap in managing conditions like valgus extension overload in overhead athletes, where nuanced tissue healing dictates return-to-play decisions. Existing segmentation challenges (e.g., IWOAI 2019) focus on gross anatomy, not on fine-detail feature evolution over time.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a supervised deep learning pipeline combining advanced denoising with feature-aware segmentation can generate novel, sensitive metrics that quantitatively track the longitudinal recovery of patellar cartilage microstructure in injured athletes, correlating more strongly with functional outcomes than standard volumetric measures.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage supervised learning framework. First, a 3D Denoising Convolutional Neural Network (DnCNN) or Noise2Variant architecture, trained on paired high-resolution/low-dose or multi-acquisition knee MRI scans, will be optimized to enhance signal-to-noise ratio while preserving subtle textural features. Second, a U-Net variant with attention gates will be trained for fine-detail segmentation, not just of the whole cartilage, but of specific pathological features (e.g., surface irregularities, deep matrix lesions) identified by radiologists. Recovery metrics will be derived from the temporal change in the segmented feature maps, such as 'fibrillation index' or 'lesion sharpness score'.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel computational pipeline for generating fine-detail recovery metrics from clinical MRI, 2) a new dataset of annotated longitudinal patellar MRI from overhead athletes with valgus extension overload, and 3) validation showing these metrics provide superior correlation with clinical recovery scores compared to traditional imaging biomarkers, advancing personalized rehabilitation monitoring.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving beyond whole-structure segmentation to the targeted quantification of transient microstructural *features* for recovery assessment, and in the tailored integration of pre-2026 denoising models specifically to enable this fine-detail tracking in noisy clinical series, a combination not explored in prior segmentation or portable sensing reviews.

proposals.title: Generalizing Deep Learning-Based Patellar Tendon Cross-Sectional Area Quantification: Validation in Cohorts with Bilateral and Absent Knee Pain
proposals.motivation: Current deep learning methods for quantifying patellar tendon cross-sectional area (CSA) from MRI, developed for injury detection, are typically validated against a unilateral pain contrast (e.g., injured vs. contralateral healthy limb). This assumption fails for common clinical populations, such as athletes with bilateral overuse syndromes (e.g., jumper's knee) or individuals undergoing screening with no pain, limiting the method's generalizability and diagnostic utility in real-world scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a supervised deep learning segmentation model, trained on expert-annotated MRI, can accurately and reliably quantify patellar tendon CSA in individuals with bilateral knee pain and pain-free controls, and that CSA differences will still be detectable between these groups and a unilateral tendinopathy cohort.
proposals.method: We will extend our established U-Net-based patellar tendon segmentation pipeline, incorporating recent advancements in attention mechanisms (e.g., squeeze-and-excitation blocks) and advanced data augmentation to improve robustness. The model will be trained on a multi-cohort dataset comprising MRI from athletes with unilateral patellar tendinopathy, bilateral pain, and pain-free controls, with ground truth from radiologist manual segmentation. Performance will be evaluated using Dice score, CSA correlation, and Bland-Altman analysis across all three cohorts to test generalizability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated, generalizable deep learning tool for patellar tendon quantification applicable to broader patient populations beyond the unilateral injury paradigm. It will establish normative CSA ranges in pain-free athletes and characterize CSA patterns in bilateral pain, directly informing clinical assessment and longitudinal monitoring of tendon health.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond the standard injured/healthy limb control model dominant in musculoskeletal imaging AI, explicitly testing and adapting a segmentation method for clinically relevant but understudied groups (bilateral pain, pain-free) where the assumption of a within-subject healthy reference is invalid.

proposals.title: Beyond the Contour: Validating Automated Patellar Cartilage Segmentation Against Functional Biomechanics in Athletes
proposals.motivation: Current validation of automated knee cartilage segmentation in MRI relies on agreement with manual tracings, which are subjective and may not reflect true biological or functional state. For clinical translation, especially in injury-prone populations like overhead athletes, segmentation-derived metrics must correlate with meaningful physical outcomes. This study addresses the critical gap between image-based computational methods and their real-world functional relevance.
proposals.hypothesis: Automated segmentation of patellar cartilage, derived from a supervised deep learning model, will yield volumetric and thickness metrics that show stronger correlation with functional knee performance and patient-reported pain outcomes than metrics derived from manual segmentation, which is prone to inter-rater variability.
proposals.method: We will train a 3D U-Net variant with attention gates on existing clinical knee MRI datasets, using weak supervision from noisy manual segmentations for initial model development. The key innovation is the validation pipeline: we will recruit a cohort of overhead athletes and correlate automated segmentation outputs (cartilage volume, mean thickness) with (1) functional biomechanical measures from portable inertial sensors during sport-specific tasks, and (2) standardized patient-reported outcome measures (KOOS). Statistical analysis will compare the correlation strength of automated vs. manual metrics against these external benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel validation framework that grounds imaging AI in functional reality, moving beyond circular comparisons to manual labels. It will deliver a clinically more relevant automated tool for patellar cartilage assessment in athletes and establish a methodology for validating medical image segmentation against non-imaging gold standards.
proposals.novelty: The proposal shifts the validation paradigm from inter-observer agreement to functional correlation, explicitly testing the assumption that manual segmentation is the best available standard. It uniquely integrates portable biomechanical sensing data as a primary validation target for a radiology AI model.

proposals.title: Adversarial Domain Adaptation for Robust Patellar Cartilage Segmentation Across MRI Scanner Manufacturers
proposals.motivation: Quantitative MRI analysis for patellar cartilage, crucial for assessing injury in athletes and osteoarthritis progression, is hindered by domain shift. MRI data from different scanner manufacturers (e.g., Siemens, GE, Philips) exhibit distinct intensity distributions and textures, degrading the performance of supervised segmentation models like U-Net trained on single-source data. This limits the clinical applicability and generalizability of automated methods developed in controlled trials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating an adversarial domain adaptation framework with a U-Net segmentation model will significantly improve segmentation accuracy and robustness on MRI data from unseen scanner manufacturers by aligning feature distributions across domains.
proposals.method: We will implement a U-Net-based segmentation network as the generator. A domain classifier (discriminator) will be trained adversarially to distinguish feature maps from source (labeled) and target (unlabeled, different scanner) domains, while the generator is simultaneously trained to segment and to fool the discriminator, promoting domain-invariant features. We will build upon the CycleGAN or AdaptSegNet framework, using paired or unpaired knee MRI datasets from public challenges (e.g., IWOAI) and clinical repositories, focusing on patellar cartilage. Training will use a combined loss: segmentation Dice loss on source data and adversarial loss for domain alignment.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robust segmentation model less sensitive to scanner variability, directly addressing a key barrier in multi-center clinical studies. It will provide a validated method for domain adaptation specifically tuned for musculoskeletal MRI, enabling more reliable cartilage quantification in heterogeneous real-world data for injury prevention and rehabilitation research.
proposals.novelty: While GANs have been used for medical image synthesis, their application for domain adaptation in patellar cartilage segmentation, specifically targeting scanner manufacturer shift in the context of athletic injury, is novel. This approach moves beyond standard supervised learning or simple augmentation used in prior work by explicitly learning a scanner-invariant representation.

proposals.title: Generative Adversarial Networks for Synthesizing Realistic Thigh MRI with Controlled Muscle and Adipose Tissue Composition
proposals.motivation: Large, annotated medical imaging datasets are scarce due to privacy concerns and annotation cost, limiting the development of robust supervised learning models for musculoskeletal analysis. Synthetic data generation offers a solution, but current methods often lack fine-grained control over tissue-specific parameters, which is critical for creating task-specific training data for segmentation or injury prediction models in athletes.
proposals.hypothesis: A conditional generative adversarial network (cGAN) can synthesize realistic, high-fidelity thigh MRI slices where key anatomical parameters, such as muscle cross-sectional area and subcutaneous adipose tissue thickness, can be explicitly controlled via conditioning vectors.
proposals.method: We will develop a StyleGAN2-ADA or a similar state-of-the-art GAN architecture, conditioned on continuous and categorical labels derived from segmentations of quadriceps, hamstrings, and adipose tissue. The model will be trained on a curated dataset of athlete thigh MRI scans, leveraging Dr. Chaudhari's expertise in knee segmentation and denoising. A key technical component will be a segmentation-based discriminator loss to enforce anatomical realism, building on recent supervised segmentation methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel tool for generating unlimited, privacy-preserving synthetic thigh MRI data with known ground truth, directly addressing data scarcity in musculoskeletal imaging research. It will enable the creation of tailored datasets for developing and benchmarking segmentation and injury risk models, particularly for athlete populations studied in Dr. Chaudhari's prior work.
proposals.novelty: While GANs have been used for medical image synthesis, applying them to the thigh with explicit, quantitative control over multiple muscle groups and adipose tissue parameters for athletic medicine is novel. This approach inverts the typical segmentation problem, using generative models to create data for supervised learning tasks.

proposals.title: Semi-Weakly Supervised Segmentation of Patellar Cartilage from Noisy Clinical MRI Using Anatomical Consistency and Self-Training
proposals.motivation: In musculoskeletal radiology, especially for patellar tracking and injury assessment in athletes, supervised deep learning models for cartilage segmentation require large datasets with pixel-perfect manual annotations, which are expensive and time-consuming to produce. Clinical MRI scans, while abundant, often contain motion artifacts, lower resolution, and inconsistent acquisition protocols, making them 'noisy' and unsuitable for training standard models. This creates a significant bottleneck for developing robust tools for quantitative analysis in sports medicine and rehabilitation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a segmentation model can be effectively trained on a mix of limited high-quality labels and abundant noisy clinical labels by enforcing anatomical consistency priors and employing a self-training paradigm to iteratively refine predictions on the noisy data.
proposals.method: We will develop a dual-branch U-Net architecture where one branch processes a small set of clean, research-grade knee MRI scans with expert segmentations, and the other processes a larger set of noisy clinical scans with only weak supervision (e.g., bounding boxes around the patella). A consistency regularization loss will be applied in a shared latent space to align feature representations. We will integrate a self-training loop where high-confidence predictions on the clinical data, filtered by an anatomically plausible shape model derived from the clean set, are iteratively added to the training set. Techniques like MixUp and label smoothing will be used to handle residual noise.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical framework for leveraging abundant, imperfect clinical imaging data for musculoskeletal biomarker development, specifically for patellar cartilage quantification. It will demonstrate a path to reduce dependency on curated research datasets, accelerating the translation of AI tools to sports medicine clinics for injury prevention and rehabilitation monitoring.
proposals.novelty: While semi-supervised learning is established, its application to noisy clinical knee MRI with anatomical consistency constraints tailored to patellar cartilage morphology is novel. The combination of weak supervision (bounding boxes) for the noisy data with a shape-prior-guided self-training loop specifically addresses the challenges of clinical radiology workflows.

ideas.title: Multi-task learning with a physics-informed auxiliary task for joint MRI reconstruction and segmentation
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from geophysical seismic imaging, specifically full-waveform inversion with facies estimation, to the domain of medical imaging. The analogous problem involved jointly reconstructing a subsurface velocity model and segmenting geological layers, where the segmentation provided structural constraints to guide the ill-posed reconstruction. The adapted idea is to develop a multi-task learning model for MRI that jointly optimizes for image reconstruction (e.g., from undersampled k-space) and semantic tissue segmentation. The network will be designed with a shared encoder that learns a latent representation from the raw or under-sampled data, branching into two task-specific decoders. The joint loss function will combine a data fidelity term (e.g., L2 norm in k-space or image domain) for reconstruction with a segmentation loss (e.g., Dice loss) for the auxiliary task. This forces the reconstruction to be informed by high-level anatomical knowledge, potentially leading to more accurate images from fewer measurements and providing immediately actionable segmentation maps without a separate processing step.

ideas.title: Clinically-Driven Stochastic Diffusion for Medical Image Reconstruction
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the continuous-time, flexible modeling of score-based SDEs with an inverted clinical validation paradigm. The core innovation is to define the forward SDE (noising process) and the reverse SDE (denoising/reconstruction process) such that the parameters of the model are optimized not for pixel-level fidelity, but to maximize the performance of a downstream clinical task predictor (e.g., tumor detection accuracy or treatment response prediction). This results in a reconstruction algorithm that inherently produces image features most salient for clinical utility, potentially tolerating or inducing specific types of noise or bias that improve diagnostic confidence, while the continuous SDE framework provides the gradient pathways necessary for this direct, end-to-end clinical optimization.

ideas.title: Benchmarking via a Standardized, Multi-Pathology Test Suite
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from computer vision, where algorithms are validated on large, diverse benchmark datasets like ImageNet, this idea proposes solving the need for broader clinical validation by creating a standardized, multi-pathology test suite for medical image denoising. The analogous solution is the use of aggregated, well-annotated datasets that represent a wide distribution of challenges. Adapted to this context, the research idea involves curating or adopting a benchmark dataset encompassing a wide variety of diseases, abnormalities, anatomical regions, and scanner-specific artifacts. The denoising method would be systematically tested on this entire suite. The evaluation metrics would extend beyond generic image quality, specifically assessing the method's robustness in preserving the subtle and varied image features critical for diagnosing each distinct clinical pathology, thereby proving its utility across a realistic clinical spectrum.

ideas.title: Cyclically Consistent, Task-Disentangled Multi-task Learning for Diagnostic Reconstruction and Segmentation
ideas.description: This system employs a modular architecture with a shared feature encoder but separate decoders for high-fidelity image reconstruction and tissue segmentation. To prevent task-specific corruption of the reconstruction, a cyclic consistency loss ensures segmentations derived from the reconstructed image match those from the original input. Furthermore, a task-discriminative adversarial loss is applied to the reconstruction decoder, forcing it to produce diagnostically complete images that are indistinguishable from those generated by a model trained solely for reconstruction, thereby preserving general utility.

ideas.title: Clinically Constrained Latent Diffusion Acceleration with Guaranteed Fidelity Bounds
ideas.description: This research develops acceleration techniques for diffusion models in clinical imaging that are governed by strict diagnostic fidelity guarantees. Instead of treating speed as the primary objective, the system uses a constrained optimization framework where inference time is minimized subject to a hard constraint that diagnostic accuracy (e.g., lesion detection rate, segmentation Dice score) does not fall below a pre-defined safety threshold relative to the original model. The approach integrates adaptive distillation and efficient sampler design with a continuous validation loop against a curated 'adversarial' dataset of edge-case medical images, ensuring robustness is not sacrificed. The final output is an accelerated model paired with a certificate of its validated performance bounds for specific clinical tasks, enabling safe and practical deployment.

ideas.title: Pathology-Aware Continuous Normalizing Flows for Robust Multi-Disease Medical Image Synthesis
ideas.description: This research proposes the development of a flow matching-based generative model specifically designed for the direct synthesis of medical images across a wide array of clinical pathologies. By conditioning the continuous normalizing flow on both anatomical context and a continuous pathology severity descriptor, the model learns to generate images along smooth, semantically meaningful trajectories from normal to diseased states for multiple conditions. This approach enables not only fast, high-fidelity data augmentation for rare diseases but also provides a novel tool for visualizing and quantifying potential disease progression pathways. The deterministic invertibility of the flow allows for precise interpolation and extrapolation in the latent pathology space, offering a robust, interpretable alternative to diffusion models for clinical validation studies.

ideas.title: A Multi-Anatomy MRI Data Commons via a Standardized Acquisition and Annotation Framework
ideas.description: The original idea to create similar datasets for brain, spine, or cardiac MRI with k-space and labels is analogous to the challenge in astronomy of creating comprehensive, usable datasets across the entire sky. Astronomy solved this through large-scale, standardized surveys like the SDSS. Adapting this solution, the research idea is to establish a 'Medical Imaging Data Commons' built on a vendor-agnostic, standardized acquisition and annotation framework. This framework would define a common protocol for capturing raw k-space data, reconstructed images, and structured pathology labels (using a unified ontology) for multiple anatomies (brain, spine, cardiac). By releasing these multi-anatomy datasets in a consistent, open format, this initiative would solve the problem of fragmented, single-anatomy datasets, enabling the development and fair comparison of generalizable MRI reconstruction and analysis algorithms across different body regions and clinical questions.

ideas.title: Active Learning for Joint Noise-Agnostic Image Restoration
ideas.description: This research direction develops an active learning framework for medical image restoration that inverts the need for a pre-defined noise map. The core model jointly estimates a clean image and a latent noise representation for each scan. The active learning strategy queries an expert annotator not for pixel-level clean labels, but for targeted, low-cost feedback (e.g., region-of-interest quality scores or sparse clean patches) on scans where the joint estimation is most uncertain or divergent. This feedback loop allows the system to efficiently bootstrap a high-performance, patient-adaptive denoiser while simultaneously building a refined understanding of the heterogeneous noise landscape across a clinical imaging cohort.

ideas.title: Anatomically-Constrained Test-Time Adaptation via a Generative Prior
ideas.description: This method performs test-time adaptation on a single scan but strictly bounds the model's updates to prevent catastrophic overfitting. It integrates a frozen, pre-trained generative model that encapsulates a prior distribution of clean, realistic anatomy. During adaptation on the noisy test input, the primary network's updates are guided by a composite loss that balances fitting the observed noisy data with a regularization term that forces the denoised output to remain on the clean anatomical manifold defined by the generative prior. This ensures the adapted model corrects for scan-specific noise without corrupting the underlying anatomical structures, leading to robust and reliable denoising.

ideas.title: Consistency-Guided Text-to-Mask Synthesis for Medical Image Segmentation
ideas.description: This research fuses vision-language models (VLMs) with consistency models to enable fast, direct synthesis of segmentation masks from radiology reports. The core innovation trains a consistency model to learn a mapping from the text embedding space of a VLM directly to the latent space of dense segmentation masks, distilling the iterative refinement process into a single step. This allows for the rapid generation of preliminary segmentation labels that are both semantically aligned with the report and anatomically consistent, serving as high-quality priors for annotation or data augmentation. The approach addresses the slow, multi-step inference of prior text-to-mask methods and eliminates the dependency on having a specific input image during the label generation phase.

ideas.title: Uncertainty-Aware, Interactive Refinement of Radiology Segmentations Using Vision-Language Model Proposals and Expert Feedback
ideas.description: This system uses a large vision-language model not for direct labeling, but to generate multiple, uncertainty-quantified segmentation hypotheses from a radiology report. These proposals are presented within an interactive interface where a radiologist can efficiently choose the best option and make precise corrections. The expert's interactions are logged to create a dataset of refined segmentations, which is then used to fine-tune a dedicated, smaller segmentation model. This approach mitigates VLM hallucination by embedding expert oversight into the workflow and creates a continuously improving model that learns from clinical feedback.

ideas.title: Meta-Learning for Fast-Adapting, Efficient Diffusion Models in Clinical Imaging
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a meta-learning framework where a diffusion model is trained to rapidly adapt its own inference process. The meta-learner's objective is to produce, from a few examples of a new scanner or protocol, a specialized, streamlined denoising pipeline—potentially via learned distillation, optimized scheduler parameters, or a pruned network architecture. This enables one-shot or few-shot customization that yields a model which is both accurate for the new domain and significantly faster than the original general-purpose model. The core synergy is that adaptation explicitly targets inference efficiency, making the model immediately practical for clinical deployment in diverse, evolving hospital environments.

ideas.title: Prospectively-Validated Metric Learning via Clinical Outcome Simulation
ideas.description: This system develops validation metrics through an iterative, simulation-informed cycle that prospectively tests for clinical utility. It first generates candidate metrics from dense labels, then uses a computational framework to simulate the prospective trial process: models are trained to optimize each candidate metric, and their performance is evaluated on a simulated 'clinical endpoint' derived from real-world data or mechanistic models. Only metrics whose optimization demonstrably and robustly improves the simulated clinical outcome prediction are advanced, creating a direct, causal link between metric design and clinical relevance before real-world deployment.

ideas.title: Developing Clinically-Aligned Validation Metrics via Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from ecology to the problem of creating clinically-aligned validation metrics for medical AI. In ecological niche modeling, a key innovation was developing validation metrics like 'transferability' that assess how well a species distribution model performs in novel geographic or climatic environments, directly linking statistical performance to the practical conservation outcome. Similarly, in medical AI, we propose to create new validation metrics that use dense, granular labels (e.g., pixel-wise segmentations, voxel-level classifications) not merely for calculating standard accuracy, but to rigorously test a model's performance in carefully constructed, clinically-relevant novel scenarios. These scenarios would be designed to mirror real-world clinical challenges—such as new imaging devices, underrepresented patient populations, or diagnostically ambiguous cases. The resulting metric would quantitatively measure a model's 'clinical robustness' or 'generalizability to clinical novelties,' providing a much stronger and more direct correlation between technical validation and ultimate clinical endpoint success than traditional benchmark performance alone.

ideas.title: A Meta-Learning Framework for Scanner Adaptation via System Identification and Few-Shot Calibration
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the system identification approach from control systems and robotics to solve the problem of scanner/protocol adaptation in medical imaging. In robotics, a new robot's parameters are quickly learned through exploratory motions (SysID). Analogously, we propose a meta-learning framework where a model performs a 'diagnostic' scan of a standardized phantom or a few unlabeled scans from a new scanner. By analyzing this minimal data, the model infers scanner-specific imaging parameters (noise, bias, resolution). This internal calibration allows the model to rapidly adapt its performance for tasks like image reconstruction or segmentation on that new scanner, achieving robust results with only a handful of subsequent labeled examples, thus enabling practical few-shot adaptation across diverse clinical imaging environments.

ideas.title: Clinical Outcome-Driven Reconstruction via Inverse Design
ideas.description: This idea inverts the clinical validation paradigm by adapting the solution from aerodynamic shape optimization. In that field, adjoint methods allow for the inverse design of aircraft wings by starting with a desired performance outcome (like minimal drag) and solving for the optimal shape. Similarly, in medical imaging reconstruction, we propose to let clinical outcomes guide the algorithm design from the outset. We would define a quantifiable clinical endpoint—such as the accuracy of detecting a specific pathology or the precision of a surgical margin—as the primary objective function. Using an inverse design or optimization framework (e.g., differentiable programming with a clinical outcome model), we would then directly solve for the reconstruction algorithm's parameters and architecture that maximize this clinical utility, effectively embedding the clinical goal into the foundational design process rather than using it only as a final evaluation step.

ideas.title: Disentangled Adversarial Priors for Joint Artifact Removal and Reconstruction
ideas.description: This approach reframes the inverse problem by explicitly separating the estimation of the pristine anatomical image from the estimation of the composite artifact field (encompassing both noise and undersampling artifacts). It uses a generator network with a dual-branch architecture to produce these two outputs, constrained by a physics-based data consistency module. An adversarial critic network is trained to discriminate between true signal components and artifact components, enforcing a clean separation and preventing the conflation of structured artifacts with anatomy. This results in a more robust model that generalizes better across varying acceleration factors, noise levels, and scanner environments.

This hybrid architecture ensures robust cross-anatomy generalization without sacrificing the precision needed for clinical diagnosis in any single domain, effectively balancing breadth and depth.

ideas.title: Apply Self-Calibration via Reference-Free Consistency for Single-Scan Adaptation
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept of test-time training by drawing an analogy from astronomical instrumentation. In astronomy, self-calibration techniques like those used in radio interferometry allow an instrument to use the observed data itself to estimate and correct for its own unique distortions, without an external calibration target. Adapting this, we propose a method where a medical imaging model performs 'self-calibration' on a single test scan. A small, adaptable sub-network is appended to a pre-trained base model. During inference, this sub-network is updated by optimizing a reference-free consistency objective derived solely from the internal structure of the test scan (e.g., enforcing that predictions are invariant to masked regions or are consistent across different spatial patches). Once calibrated, this module transforms the features to better fit the specific noise, contrast, and artifact characteristics of that individual scan, leading to more robust segmentation or reconstruction from a single, potentially noisy, test example.

ideas.title: Geophysical Tomography via Neural Implicit Representations
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept of using neural rendering for MRI by drawing an analogy from geophysical seismic tomography. In that field, implicit neural representations are used to reconstruct a continuous 3D subsurface velocity model from sparse, irregular seismic travel-time data, constrained by the physics of wave propagation. Adapted to MRI, the method involves training a neural radiance field (NeRF) or similar implicit representation to model the continuous 3D spatial image. The key adaptation is that the network is trained to directly map spatial coordinates to tissue properties, while its Fourier transform is constrained to match the acquired sparse k-space samples. This incorporates the physical forward model (Fourier transform) as a hard constraint during training, enabling the synthesis of a complete, high-fidelity 3D volume from a highly undersampled k-space acquisition, effectively performing novel 'view' (or contrast) synthesis directly from the raw measurement domain.

ideas.title: Patch-Level Joint Noise-Content Diffusion for Edge-Based Real-Time Image Enhancement
ideas.description: This research develops a novel diffusion architecture designed for training on small image patches, where the model learns to jointly predict both the clean patch and its inherent noise level in a single forward pass. By inverting the need for a separate noise map or schedule, the model becomes self-contained and can adapt to the unknown, potentially non-uniform noise present in individual patches captured by edge devices like medical scanners. This results in a dramatically simplified and faster pipeline ideal for deployment, where the model performs real-time, blind enhancement directly on the sensor's output patches without external noise estimation overhead.

ideas.title: Active Learning for Scan Annotation via Query-by-Committee and Uncertainty Sampling
ideas.description: This idea adapts an active learning solution from computational chemistry—specifically, the Query-by-Committee (QBC) method used in virtual screening for drug discovery—to the problem of efficiently selecting medical scans for expert annotation. In the analogous field, an ensemble of predictive models selects molecules for costly experimental assays based on maximal disagreement among the committee, optimizing the information gained per expensive experiment. Adapted to this context, an ensemble of diverse deep learning models is trained on a seed set of annotated scans. The system then prioritizes for expert annotation those unlabeled scans where the model committee's predictions show the highest disagreement (uncertainty). This approach directly targets the scans that are most ambiguous to the current models, thereby improving model performance with the fewest possible expert annotations.

ideas.title: Uncertainty-Driven Adaptive MRI with Diagnostic Utility Optimization
ideas.description: This system moves beyond simple real-time reconstruction to incorporate a probabilistic model that quantifies spatial and feature-wise uncertainty in the ongoing scan. It frames sampling as a sequential decision problem, where each potential k-space acquisition is evaluated for its expected contribution to reducing diagnostic uncertainty. The adaptive protocol is therefore explicitly optimized to rule out critical pathologies or confirm normal anatomy, ensuring robust performance across diverse clinical presentations. This transforms the scanner from a passive imager into an active diagnostic agent that minimizes the risk of missing subtle but clinically significant findings.

ideas.title: Physics-Grounded Meta-Learning for Robust Cross-Scanner Adaptation
ideas.description: This system combines meta-learning with an explicit, differentiable forward model of MR image formation to enable robust adaptation to unseen scanners. A meta-learned network is trained to quickly infer the scanner-specific parameters of this physics model from a few calibration shots. Once these parameters are estimated, the forward model can simulate data from the new scanner, allowing the task network to adapt reliably even when the new scanner's properties are outside the original meta-training distribution. This approach ensures adaptation is constrained by physical reality, preventing catastrophic failures on fundamentally novel devices.

ideas.title: Anatomically-Conditioned Hierarchical Diffusion for Edge Deployment
ideas.description: This approach trains an ultra-fast, edge-deployable diffusion model by decoupling global anatomy from local texture generation. A compact convolutional network first processes a downsampled input or noise to produce a low-resolution latent map encoding anatomical layout and structural boundaries. A patch-based diffusion model then performs denoising, conditioned on this latent map, to generate high-fidelity local details that are globally consistent. This hierarchical conditioning ensures the final output is both computationally efficient and anatomically plausible, making it suitable for real-time applications in the scanner.

ideas.title: Mapping the Information-Theoretic Frontier for Task-Specific Utility in Extreme Data Regimes
ideas.description: This study moves beyond ad-hoc 'extreme' testing to rigorously map the conditions under which a computational imaging or inference method retains task-specific utility. Data is progressively degraded along calibrated axes of noise and undersampling until a quantitative performance metric (e.g., classification accuracy, structural similarity, parameter estimation error) falls below a predefined application-relevant threshold. The resulting failure boundary is analyzed to determine if performance collapse is due to the method's specific architecture/prior or aligns with an estimated information-theoretic limit for the task, providing a clear diagnostic of both algorithmic robustness and fundamental data requirements.

ideas.title: Adaptive Schedule Learning within Score-Based SDEs for Efficient Continuous-Time Diffusion
ideas.description: This approach augments the continuous-time score-based SDE framework by making the noise schedule a learnable component of the model. A neural network parameterizes the drift and diffusion coefficients of the forward SDE, which are optimized concurrently with the score network during training. This allows the model to discover a noise schedule that optimally bridges the data and noise distributions for the specific dataset, minimizing the complexity of the reverse diffusion path. The result is a more robust and sampling-efficient generative model that retains the flexibility of the continuous-time formulation while mitigating the sensitivity to heuristic schedule design.

ideas.title: Diversity-Guarded Active Learning for Robust Annotation
ideas.description: This system combines uncertainty-based active learning with explicit diversity constraints to ensure selected scans are both informative for the model and representative of the overall data distribution. It employs a hybrid acquisition function that balances a traditional uncertainty score (e.g., entropy) with a density or coverage score derived from a feature space model of all unlabeled data. This prevents the annotation effort from over-concentrating on a peculiar, hard-to-label subset and ensures the resulting training set supports a model that generalizes well. The approach is designed to be robust to initial model bias and noisy oracle feedback, systematically building a comprehensive and useful annotated dataset.

ideas.title: 3D Volumetric Flow Matching for Direct and Coherent Generation
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a novel generative framework that applies flow matching directly to 3D volumetric data. Instead of learning to denoise a 3D volume through iterative diffusion steps, the model learns a continuous, deterministic path from a simple prior distribution (e.g., Gaussian) to the complex data distribution of 3D volumes in a single, straight-line flow. This approach inherently models cross-slice and 3D spatial correlations within the flow dynamics, ensuring globally coherent structure generation. The result is a more efficient and stable alternative to 3D diffusion models, capable of direct, high-quality volumetric synthesis for applications like medical imaging and material science.

ideas.title: Clinically-Guided Interactive Reconstruction for Ambiguous Medical Queries
ideas.description: This research inverts the validation paradigm by using downstream clinical outcomes (e.g., diagnostic accuracy, treatment efficacy) not merely to score, but to fundamentally design how LLMs handle underspecified clinical instructions. Instead of treating ambiguity as a noise source, the system learns which clarification questions to ask by optimizing for the clinical utility of the final, reconstructed answer. This results in a novel class of interactive models where the dialogue strategy for disambiguation is co-evolved with the answer-generation algorithm, both being shaped by the same clinical outcome signal to ensure the collaborative human-AI interaction directly improves patient care.

ideas.title: Self-Consistent, Physics-Constrained Joint Estimation of Image and Noise
ideas.description: This method jointly estimates a clean image and a corresponding noise map without a separate pre-estimation step, but crucially avoids ill-posedness by enforcing a hard consistency constraint derived from a physical noise model. A neural network with intertwined branches produces the two outputs, which are then fed into a differentiable forward model that re-synthesizes a noisy image. The system is trained to minimize the discrepancy between the original noisy input and this re-synthesized image, ensuring the noise map and clean estimate are mutually plausible. This creates a robust, self-supervised framework where the noise model itself provides the necessary regularization for stable and accurate joint estimation.

ideas.title: Adaptive MRI via Real-Time Feedback Control Inspired by Robotic Motion Planning
ideas.description: This idea challenges the static assumption of pre-defined MRI acquisition protocols by drawing an analogy from robotic motion planning. In robotics, systems like RRT and MPC use real-time sensor feedback to dynamically replan paths toward a goal, avoiding obstacles. The adapted solution applies this feedback control principle to MRI. An initial, very fast undersampled acquisition provides a preliminary reconstruction. A real-time analysis module then evaluates this image, identifying areas of high uncertainty or diagnostic interest. This information feeds into a sampling planner that dynamically selects the next most informative k-space points or trajectories to acquire, creating a closed-loop, adaptive MRI system. The protocol evolves during the scan, optimizing for specific image features, motion states, or suspected pathologies, thereby maximizing information content per unit time and moving beyond one-size-fits-all static sequences.

ideas.title: Adapting Blind Source Separation for Spatially-Varying MRI Noise
ideas.description: This idea challenges the common MRI reconstruction assumption of stationary, Gaussian noise by adapting solutions from geophysics. In seismic processing, blind source separation methods like Independent Component Analysis are used to disentangle desired signals from complex, spatially varying noise without prior noise models. The adapted research idea applies this framework to MRI by treating the acquired k-space or image data as a mixture of the true MR signal and multiple independent noise source processes (e.g., from coil elements, physiological motion, or system imperfections). An algorithm would be developed to statistically separate these components by maximizing their independence, thereby learning a spatially adaptive and potentially non-Gaussian noise model directly from the acquisition data. This would enable more robust denoising and reconstruction for real-world MRI systems where noise characteristics vary across the field of view and deviate from simple Gaussian distributions.

ideas.title: Adaptive Step-Size Control for Diffusion Model Inference via Analogous ODE Solver Techniques
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to optimize inference speed for clinical deployment of diffusion models by drawing an analogy from numerical methods in Computational Fluid Dynamics. The analogous problem is solving stiff Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs) efficiently, which is addressed by adaptive step-size solvers that vary the integration step based on local error estimates to balance speed and accuracy. The adapted idea is to develop a novel sampling technique for diffusion models that incorporates adaptive step-size control. By dynamically adjusting the denoising step size according to an estimated rate of change in the latent distribution, the method would minimize the number of required sampling steps for a given quality threshold, directly reducing denoising time for practical clinical inference.

ideas.title: Joint State-Parameter Estimation for Image Denoising
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a noise map must be explicitly estimated as a separate step before or during denoising. Drawing an analogy from geophysics, specifically seismic tomography's joint state-parameter estimation (like Full Waveform Inversion for source and structure), we propose a method for image denoising that inverts the noisy image directly for the clean signal. The adapted solution is a unified model—potentially a novel neural architecture or optimization framework—that treats the clean image and the noise characteristics (level, possibly type) as coupled latent variables. It is trained or formulated to minimize reconstruction error, thereby jointly inferring both the denoised result and the underlying noise parameters in a single, end-to-end process, effectively making the explicit noise map an unnecessary intermediate product.

ideas.title: Adapting Multi-Spectral Image Fusion for Multi-Shell Diffusion Denoising
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of focusing denoising on single-shell data by drawing an analogy from remote sensing. In multi-spectral imaging, fusion techniques combine noisy data from different wavelengths to create a superior composite. Similarly, we propose to treat multi-shell diffusion MRI data as a set of complementary 'contrast' channels (like spectral bands). The adapted solution is to implement a multi-resolution fusion framework (e.g., using wavelets or a neural network architecture) for denoising. This framework would decompose each shell's signal, intelligently integrate information across shells and spatial scales based on local noise and signal properties, and reconstruct a denoised multi-shell dataset. This directly preserves the complex, multi-shell relationships crucial for advanced biophysical models, moving beyond single-shell or sequential processing approaches.

ideas.title: 3D Volumetric Denoising via Seismic Wavefield Reconstruction
ideas.description: This idea extends the framework to denoise entire 3D diffusion volumes jointly by drawing an analogy from geophysical seismic imaging. In seismic processing, 5D interpolation and joint reconstruction techniques treat noisy, incomplete 3D wavefield data as a high-dimensional tensor, exploiting correlations across all dimensions via low-rank or tensor completion methods. Adapted to this context, the diffusion MRI volume (spatial dimensions x, y, z and diffusion encoding dimension b) is modeled as a single 4D/5D tensor. A denoising solution is then implemented via a constrained optimization, such as tensor nuclear norm minimization or a learned tensor dictionary decomposition, which captures cross-slice and cross-encoding correlations simultaneously to reconstruct the clean volumetric data, moving beyond independent slice-wise processing.
